义勇军进行曲 *Yìyǒngjūn jìnxíngqǔ* "[March of the Volunteers](/wiki/March_of_the_Volunteers "March of the Volunteers")" | |

(**PRC**),[[i]](#cite_note-20) is a country in [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"). It is the [second-most populous country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_(United_Nations) "List of countries by population (United Nations)") after India, with [a population](/wiki/Population_of_China "Population of China") exceeding 1.4 billion, representing 17% of the world's population. China [borders fourteen countries by land](/wiki/Borders_of_China "Borders of China")[[j]](#cite_note-21) across an area of 9.6 million square kilometers (3,700,000 sq mi), making it the [third-largest country by area](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area").[[k]](#cite_note-24) The country is divided into 33 [province-level divisions](/wiki/Province-level_divisions_of_China "Province-level divisions of China"): 22 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_China "Provinces of China"),[[l]](#cite_note-25) 5 [autonomous regions](/wiki/Autonomous_regions_of_China "Autonomous regions of China"), 4 [municipalities](/wiki/Direct-administered_municipalities_of_China "Direct-administered municipalities of China"), and 2 semi-autonomous [special administrative regions](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions "Special administrative regions"). [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") is the capital, while [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") is the [most populous city by urban area](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_China_by_population "List of cities in China by population") and largest [financial center](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center").

**China**,[[h]](#cite_note-19) officially the **People's Republic of China** (**PRC**),[[i]](#cite_note-20) is a country in [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"). It is the [second-most populous country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_(United_Nations) "List of countries by population (United Nations)") after India, with [a population](/wiki/Population_of_China "Population of China") exceeding 1.4 billion, representing 17% of the world's population. China [borders fourteen countries by land](/wiki/Borders_of_China "Borders of China")[[j]](#cite_note-21) across an area of 9.6 million square kilometers (3,700,000 sq mi), making it the [third-largest country by area](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area").[[k]](#cite_note-24) The country is divided into 33 [province-level divisions](/wiki/Province-level_divisions_of_China "Province-level divisions of China"): 22 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_China "Provinces of China"),[[l]](#cite_note-25) 5 [autonomous regions](/wiki/Autonomous_regions_of_China "Autonomous regions of China"), 4 [municipalities](/wiki/Direct-administered_municipalities_of_China "Direct-administered municipalities of China"), and 2 semi-autonomous [special administrative regions](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions "Special administrative regions"). [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") is the capital, while [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") is the [most populous city by urban area](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_China_by_population "List of cities in China by population") and largest [financial center](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center").

The first humans in China arrived during the [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") era. By the 2nd millennium BCE [dynastic states](/wiki/Dynasties_in_Chinese_history "Dynasties in Chinese history") had emerged in the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") basin. The 8th–3rd centuries BCE saw a breakdown in the authority of the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty"), accompanied by the emergence of administrative and military techniques, [literature](/wiki/Chinese_classics "Chinese classics") and [philosophy](/wiki/Chinese_philosophy "Chinese philosophy"). In 221 BCE, China was unified under [an emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_China "Emperor of China"), ushering in two millennia of imperial rule. Chinese achievements include the invention of [gunpowder](/wiki/Invention_of_gunpowder "Invention of gunpowder"), [paper](/wiki/History_of_paper#Paper_in_China "History of paper"), [printing](/wiki/Printing "Printing") and the [compass](/wiki/Compass "Compass"), the establishment of the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"), and the building of the [Great Wall](/wiki/Great_Wall "Great Wall"). [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture") has flourished and has had a great influence on the [region and beyond](/wiki/Sinosphere "Sinosphere"). China began to cede [parts of the country](/wiki/Foreign_concessions_in_China "Foreign concessions in China") in the 19th century, to European powers through a series of [unequal treaties](/wiki/Unequal_treaties "Unequal treaties"). The [1911 Revolution](/wiki/1911_Revolution "1911 Revolution") overthrew the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") and the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") was established the following year. The country was unstable and fragmented during the [Warlord Era](/wiki/Warlord_Era "Warlord Era"), this came to an end with the [Northern Expedition](/wiki/Northern_Expedition "Northern Expedition") conducted by the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") to reunify the country.

The [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") began in 1927, when Kuomintang forces [purged](/wiki/Shanghai_massacre "Shanghai massacre") members of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP). China was invaded by the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") in 1937, leading the CCP and Kuomintang to form the [Second United Front](/wiki/Second_United_Front "Second United Front") to fight the Japanese. The [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") ended in a Chinese victory; however, the CCP and the Kuomintang resumed their civil war. In 1949, the CCP [proclaimed the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China") and forced the [Kuomintang-led government to retreat](/wiki/Retreat_of_the_government_of_the_Republic_of_China_to_Taiwan "Retreat of the government of the Republic of China to Taiwan") to the [island of Taiwan](/wiki/Island_of_Taiwan "Island of Taiwan"). The country was split, with [both sides](/wiki/Two_Chinas "Two Chinas") claiming to be the [legitimate government](/wiki/One_China "One China"). Following the implementation of [land reforms](/wiki/Land_Reform_Movement "Land Reform Movement"), attempts by the CCP to realize [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") failed: the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward") was responsible for the [Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine") which resulted in millions of deaths, and the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution") was a period of turmoil and persecution. The [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up") that began in 1978 moved the country away from a [planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") towards a [market-based economy](/wiki/Socialist_market_economy "Socialist market economy"), spurring an economic boom. A movement for political liberalization stalled after the [1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests_and_massacre "1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre").

Since 1949, China has been a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") communist state with the CCP as its [sole ruling party](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state"). It is one of the five [permanent members](/wiki/Permanent_members_of_the_United_Nations_Security_Council "Permanent members of the United Nations Security Council") of the [UN Security Council](/wiki/UN_Security_Council "UN Security Council") and a member of [numerous multilateral and regional organizations](/wiki/International_organization_membership_of_China "International organization membership of China"). Making up around one-fifth of the world's economy, China is the [second-wealthiest country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_total_wealth#Total_wealth_by_country "List of countries by total wealth") in the world, with the [Chinese economy](/wiki/Chinese_economy "Chinese economy") being the largest when adjusted for [PPP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)"). However, China [ranks poorly](/wiki/International_rankings_of_China "International rankings of China") in measures of [democracy](/wiki/Democracy_in_China "Democracy in China") and [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights_in_China "Human rights in China"). China has been one of the [fastest-growing](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_real_GDP_growth_rate "List of countries by real GDP growth rate") modern economies and is the world's [largest manufacturer](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_manufacturing_output "List of countries by manufacturing output") and [exporter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports"), as well as the [second-largest importer](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports "List of countries by imports"). China has the world's [largest standing army](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_military_and_paramilitary_personnel "List of countries by number of military and paramilitary personnel"), [second-largest defense budget](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures"), and [third-largest nuclear weapons stockpile](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_China "Nuclear weapons of China"). It is described as either a [potential or established superpower](/wiki/Potential_superpower#China "Potential superpower") due to its influence in the fields of geopolitics, [science and technology](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_China "Science and technology in China"), [manufacturing](/wiki/Industry_in_China "Industry in China"), economics and culture. China is known for its [cuisine](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine") and [culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture"). It is a [megadiverse country](/wiki/Megadiverse_country "Megadiverse country"), and has [60 UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_China "List of World Heritage Sites in China").

## Etymology

Main article: [Names of China](/wiki/Names_of_China "Names of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/CEM-09-Asiae-Nova-Descriptio-China-2510.jpg/250px-CEM-09-Asiae-Nova-Descriptio-China-2510.jpg)](/wiki/File:CEM-09-Asiae-Nova-Descriptio-China-2510.jpg)

*China* (today's [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong")), *Mangi* (inland of *[Xanton](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong")*), and *Cataio* (inland of *China* and *[Chequan](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang")*, and including the capital *[Cambalu](/wiki/Khanbaliq "Khanbaliq")*, *[Xandu](/wiki/Shangdu "Shangdu")*, and a [marble bridge](/wiki/Marco_Polo_Bridge "Marco Polo Bridge")) are all shown as separate regions on this 1570 map by [Abraham Ortelius](/wiki/Abraham_Ortelius "Abraham Ortelius").

The word "China" has been used in English since the 16th century; however, it was not used by the Chinese themselves during this period. Its origin has been traced through [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"), and [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") back to the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") word चिन *Cīna*, used in [ancient India](/wiki/Ancient_India "Ancient India").[[14]](#cite_note-OED-26) "China" appears in [Richard Eden](/wiki/Richard_Eden_(translator) "Richard Eden (translator)")'s 1555 translation[[m]](#cite_note-29) of the 1516 journal of the Portuguese explorer [Duarte Barbosa](/wiki/Duarte_Barbosa "Duarte Barbosa").[[n]](#cite_note-32)[[14]](#cite_note-OED-26) Barbosa's usage was derived from Persian *Chīn* ([چین](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%DA%86%DB%8C%D9%86 "wikt:چین")), which in turn derived from Sanskrit *[Cīna](/wiki/Chinas "Chinas")* ([चीन](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%A4%9A%E0%A5%80%E0%A4%A8 "wikt:चीन")).[[19]](#cite_note-AmHer-33) The origin of the Sanskrit word is a matter of debate.[[14]](#cite_note-OED-26) *Cīna* was first used in early [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") scripture, including the *[Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")* (3rd century BCE–4th century CE) and the *[Laws of Manu](/wiki/Manusmriti "Manusmriti")* (2nd century BCE–2nd century CE).[[20]](#cite_note-wade-34) In 1655, [Martino Martini](/wiki/Martino_Martini "Martino Martini") suggested that the word China is derived ultimately from the name of the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BCE) or the prior state of [Qin](/wiki/Qin_(state) "Qin (state)").[[21]](#cite_note-Martini-35)[[20]](#cite_note-wade-34) Although use in Indian sources precedes this dynasty, though not the state, this derivation is still given in various sources.[[22]](#cite_note-Bodde-1978-36) Alternative suggestions include the names for [Yelang](/wiki/Yelang "Yelang") and the [Jing](/wiki/Chu_(state) "Chu (state)") or Chu state.[[20]](#cite_note-wade-34)[[23]](#cite_note-Yule-1866-37)

The official name of the modern state is the "People's Republic of China" ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): [中华人民共和国](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E5%8D%8E%E4%BA%BA%E6%B0%91%E5%85%B1%E5%92%8C%E5%9B%BD "wikt:中华人民共和国"); [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): [中華人民共和國](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E8%8F%AF%E4%BA%BA%E6%B0%91%E5%85%B1%E5%92%8C%E5%9C%8B "wikt:中華人民共和國"); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Zhōnghuá rénmín gònghéguó*). The shorter form is "China" ([中国](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD "wikt:中国"); [中國](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E5%9C%8B "wikt:中國"); *Zhōngguó*), from *zhōng* ('central') and *guó* ('state'), a term which developed under the [Western Zhou](/wiki/Western_Zhou "Western Zhou") dynasty in reference to its [royal demesne](/wiki/Demesne "Demesne").[[o]](#cite_note-39)[[p]](#cite_note-41) It was used in official documents as an synonym for the state under the [Qing](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty").[[26]](#cite_note-wilx-42) The name *Zhongguo* is also translated as 'Middle Kingdom' in English.[[27]](#cite_note-Tang-2010-43) China is sometimes referred to as [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") or "the Mainland" when distinguishing it from the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China") or the [PRC's Special Administrative Regions](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_China "Special administrative regions of China").[[28]](#cite_note-44)[[29]](#cite_note-45)[[30]](#cite_note-asia-34729538-46)

## History

Main article: [History of China](/wiki/History_of_China "History of China")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Chinese history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Chinese_history "Timeline of Chinese history").

### Prehistory

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/National_Museum_of_China_2014.02.01_14-43-38.jpg/250px-National_Museum_of_China_2014.02.01_14-43-38.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Museum_of_China_2014.02.01_14-43-38.jpg)

10,000-year-old pottery, [Xianren Cave](/wiki/Xianren_Cave "Xianren Cave") culture (18000–7000 BCE)

[Archaeological evidence](/wiki/Archaeological_excavation "Archaeological excavation") suggests that early [hominids](/wiki/Hominidae "Hominidae") inhabited China 2.25 million years ago.[[31]](#cite_note-47) The hominid fossils of [Peking Man](/wiki/Peking_Man "Peking Man"), a *[Homo erectus](/wiki/Homo_erectus "Homo erectus")* who [used fire](/wiki/Control_of_fire_by_early_humans "Control of fire by early humans"),[[32]](#cite_note-48) have been dated to between 680,000 and 780,000 [years ago](/wiki/Before_Present "Before Present").[[33]](#cite_note-autogenerated198-49) The fossilized teeth of *Homo sapiens* (dated to 125,000–80,000 years ago) have been discovered in [Fuyan Cave](/wiki/Fuyan_Cave "Fuyan Cave").[[34]](#cite_note-50) Chinese [proto-writing](/wiki/Proto-writing "Proto-writing") existed in [Jiahu](/wiki/Jiahu "Jiahu") around 6600 BCE,[[35]](#cite_note-earliest_writing-51) at [Damaidi](/wiki/Damaidi "Damaidi") around 6000 BCE,[[36]](#cite_note-52) [Dadiwan](/wiki/Dadiwan_culture "Dadiwan culture") from 5800 to 5400 BCE, and [Banpo](/wiki/Banpo "Banpo") dating from the 5th millennium BCE. Some scholars have suggested that the [Jiahu symbols](/wiki/Jiahu_symbols "Jiahu symbols") (7th millennium BCE) constituted the earliest Chinese writing system.[[35]](#cite_note-earliest_writing-51)

### Early dynastic rule

Further information: [Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors](/wiki/Three_Sovereigns_and_Five_Emperors "Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors"), [Xia dynasty](/wiki/Xia_dynasty "Xia dynasty"), [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty"), [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty"), [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period"), and [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/26120-Anyang_%2849086436522%29.jpg/250px-26120-Anyang_%2849086436522%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:26120-Anyang_(49086436522).jpg)

[Yinxu](/wiki/Yinxu "Yinxu"), the ruins of the capital of the late [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") (14th century BCE)

According to traditional [Chinese historiography](/wiki/Chinese_historiography "Chinese historiography"), the [Xia dynasty](/wiki/Xia_dynasty "Xia dynasty") was established during the late 3rd millennium BCE, marking the beginning of the dynastic cycle that was understood to underpin China's entire political history. In the modern era, the Xia's historicity came under increasing scrutiny, in part due to the earliest known attestation of the Xia being written millennia after the date given for their collapse. In 1958, archaeologists discovered sites belonging to the [Erlitou culture](/wiki/Erlitou_culture "Erlitou culture") that existed during the early [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"); they have since been characterized as the remains of the historical Xia, but this conception is often rejected.[[37]](#cite_note-53)[[38]](#cite_note-54)[[39]](#cite_note-55) The [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") that traditionally succeeded the Xia is the earliest for which there are both contemporary written records and undisputed archaeological evidence.[[40]](#cite_note-56) The Shang ruled much of the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") valley until the 11th century BCE, with the earliest hard evidence dated c. 1300 BCE.[[41]](#cite_note-57) The [oracle bone script](/wiki/Oracle_bone_script "Oracle bone script"), attested from c. 1250 BCE but generally assumed to be considerably older,[[42]](#cite_note-58)[[43]](#cite_note-59) represents the oldest known form of [written Chinese](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese"),[[44]](#cite_note-60) and is the direct ancestor of modern [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters").[[45]](#cite_note-61)

The Shang were overthrown by the [Zhou](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty"), who ruled between the 11th and 5th centuries BCE, though the centralized authority of [Son of Heaven](/wiki/Son_of_Heaven "Son of Heaven") was slowly eroded by *[fengjian](/wiki/Fengjian "Fengjian")* lords. Some principalities eventually emerged from the weakened Zhou and continually waged war with each other during the 300-year [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period"). By the time of the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period") of the 5th–3rd centuries BCE, there were seven major powerful states left.[[46]](#cite_note-62)

### Imperial China

Further information: [Chinese Empire](/wiki/Chinese_Empire "Chinese Empire") and [History of China § Imperial China](/wiki/History_of_China#Imperial_China "History of China")

#### Qin and Han

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Han_Expansion.png/330px-Han_Expansion.png)](/wiki/File:Han_Expansion.png)

The [southward expansion of the Han dynasty](/wiki/Southward_expansion_of_the_Han_dynasty "Southward expansion of the Han dynasty") during the 2nd century BCE

The Warring States period ended in 221 BCE after the [state of Qin](/wiki/Qin_(state) "Qin (state)") conquered the other six states, reunited China and established the dominant order of [autocracy](/wiki/Autocracy "Autocracy"). [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang") proclaimed himself the Emperor of the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty"), becoming the first emperor of a unified China. He enacted [legalist](/wiki/Legalism_(Chinese_philosophy) "Legalism (Chinese philosophy)") reforms, notably the standardization of Chinese characters, [measurements](/wiki/Chinese_units_of_measurement "Chinese units of measurement"), road widths, and [currency](/wiki/History_of_Chinese_currency "History of Chinese currency"). His dynasty also [conquered the Yue tribes](/wiki/Qin_campaign_against_the_Baiyue "Qin campaign against the Baiyue") in [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi"), [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), and [Northern Vietnam](/wiki/Northern_Vietnam "Northern Vietnam").[[47]](#cite_note-63) The Qin dynasty lasted only fifteen years, falling soon after the Qin Shi Huang's death.[[48]](#cite_note-Bodde1986-64)[[49]](#cite_note-Lewis2007-65)

Following [widespread revolts](/wiki/Chu%E2%80%93Han_Contention "Chu–Han Contention") during which the imperial library [was burned](/wiki/List_of_destroyed_libraries#Human_action "List of destroyed libraries"),[[q]](#cite_note-67) the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") emerged to rule China between 206 BCE and 220 CE, creating a cultural identity among its populace still remembered in the ethnonym of the modern [Han Chinese](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese").[[48]](#cite_note-Bodde1986-64)[[49]](#cite_note-Lewis2007-65) The Han [expanded the empire's territory considerably](/wiki/History_of_the_Han_dynasty "History of the Han dynasty"), with military campaigns reaching [Central Asia, Mongolia](/wiki/Han%E2%80%93Xiongnu_Wars "Han–Xiongnu Wars"), [Korea](/wiki/Han_conquest_of_Gojoseon "Han conquest of Gojoseon"), and [Yunnan](/wiki/Han_conquest_of_Dian "Han conquest of Dian"), and the [recovery of Guangdong and northern Vietnam](/wiki/Southward_expansion_of_the_Han_dynasty "Southward expansion of the Han dynasty") from [Nanyue](/wiki/Nanyue "Nanyue"). Han involvement in Central Asia and [Sogdia](/wiki/Sogdia "Sogdia") helped establish the land route of the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"), replacing the earlier path over the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") to India. Han China gradually became the largest economy of the ancient world.[[51]](#cite_note-Dahlman_Aubert_2001-68) Despite the Han's initial decentralization and the official abandonment of the Qin philosophy of Legalism in favor of [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), Qin's legalist institutions and policies continued to be employed by the Han government and its successors.[[52]](#cite_note-69)

#### Three Kingdoms, Jin, Northern and Southern dynasties

After the [end of the Han dynasty](/wiki/End_of_the_Han_dynasty "End of the Han dynasty"), a period of strife known as [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms") followed, at the end of which [Wei](/wiki/Cao_Wei "Cao Wei") was swiftly overthrown by the [Jin dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(266%E2%80%93420) "Jin dynasty (266–420)"). The Jin fell to [civil war](/wiki/War_of_the_Eight_Princes "War of the Eight Princes") upon the ascension of the developmentally disabled [Emperor Hui](/wiki/Emperor_Hui_of_Jin "Emperor Hui of Jin"); the [Five Barbarians](/wiki/Five_Barbarians "Five Barbarians") then [rebelled](/wiki/Upheaval_of_the_Five_Barbarians "Upheaval of the Five Barbarians") and ruled northern China as the [Sixteen States](/wiki/Sixteen_Kingdoms "Sixteen Kingdoms"). The [Xianbei](/wiki/Xianbei "Xianbei") unified them as the [Northern Wei](/wiki/Northern_Wei "Northern Wei"), whose [Emperor Xiaowen](/wiki/Emperor_Xiaowen_of_Northern_Wei "Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei") reversed his predecessors' apartheid policies and [enforced a drastic sinification on his subjects](/wiki/Northern_dynasties "Northern dynasties"). In the south, the general [Liu Yu](/wiki/Emperor_Wu_of_Song "Emperor Wu of Song") secured the abdication of the Jin in favor of the [Liu Song](/wiki/Liu_Song_dynasty "Liu Song dynasty"). The various successors of these states became known as the [Northern and Southern dynasties](/wiki/Northern_and_Southern_dynasties "Northern and Southern dynasties"), with the two areas finally reunited by the [Sui](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty") in 589.[[53]](#cite_note-70)[[54]](#cite_note-71)

#### Sui, Tang and Song

The Sui restored the Han to power through China, reformed its agriculture, economy and [imperial examination](/wiki/Imperial_examination "Imperial examination") system, constructed the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_of_China "Grand Canal of China"), and patronized [Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism"). However, they fell quickly when their conscription for public works and a [failed war](/wiki/Goguryeo%E2%80%93Sui_War "Goguryeo–Sui War") in [northern Korea](/wiki/Goguryeo "Goguryeo") provoked widespread unrest.[[55]](#cite_note-72)[[56]](#cite_note-73)
Under the succeeding [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") and [Song dynasties](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), Chinese economy, technology, and culture entered a golden age.[[57]](#cite_note-74) The Tang dynasty retained control of the [Western Regions](/wiki/Western_Regions "Western Regions") and the Silk Road,[[58]](#cite_note-75) which brought traders to as far as [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") and the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa"),[[59]](#cite_note-76) and made the capital [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an") a cosmopolitan urban center. However, it was devastated and weakened by the [An Lushan rebellion](/wiki/An_Lushan_rebellion "An Lushan rebellion") in the 8th century.[[60]](#cite_note-77) In 907, the Tang disintegrated completely when the local military governors became ungovernable. The Song dynasty ended the [separatist situation](/wiki/Five_Dynasties_and_Ten_Kingdoms_period "Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period") in 960, leading to a balance of power between the Song and the [Liao dynasty](/wiki/Liao_dynasty "Liao dynasty"). The Song was the first government in world history to issue paper money and the first Chinese polity to establish a permanent navy which was supported by the developed shipbuilding industry along with the sea trade.[[61]](#cite_note-78)

Between the 10th and 11th century CE, the population of China doubled to around 100 million people, mostly because of the expansion of rice cultivation in central and southern China, and the production of abundant food surpluses. The Song dynasty also saw a [revival of Confucianism](/wiki/Neo-Confucianism "Neo-Confucianism"), in response to the growth of Buddhism during the Tang,[[62]](#cite_note-79) and a flourishing of philosophy and the arts, as [landscape art](/wiki/Landscape_art "Landscape art") and [porcelain](/wiki/Porcelain "Porcelain") were brought to new levels of complexity.[[63]](#cite_note-80) However, the military weakness of the Song army was observed by the [Jin dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(1115%E2%80%931234) "Jin dynasty (1115–1234)"). In 1127, [Emperor Emeritus Huizong](/wiki/Emperor_Huizong_of_Song "Emperor Huizong of Song"), [Emperor Qinzong](/wiki/Emperor_Qinzong "Emperor Qinzong") and the capital [Bianjing](/wiki/Kaifeng "Kaifeng") were captured during the [Jin–Song wars](/wiki/Jin%E2%80%93Song_wars "Jin–Song wars"). The remnants of the Song retreated to [southern China](/wiki/Northern_and_southern_China "Northern and southern China") and reestablished [the Song](/wiki/Southern_Song_Dynasty "Southern Song Dynasty") at [Jiankang](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing").[[64]](#cite_note-81)

#### Yuan, Ming and Qing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Badaling_China_Great-Wall-of-China-01.jpg/330px-Badaling_China_Great-Wall-of-China-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Badaling_China_Great-Wall-of-China-01.jpg)

China's first emperor, [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang"), is famed for having united the [Warring States](/wiki/Warring_States "Warring States")' walls to form the [Great Wall of China](/wiki/Great_Wall_of_China "Great Wall of China"). Most of the present structure dates to the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty").

The [Mongol conquest of China](/wiki/Mongol_conquest_of_China "Mongol conquest of China") began in 1205 with the [campaigns](/wiki/Mongol_conquest_of_Western_Xia "Mongol conquest of Western Xia") against [Western Xia](/wiki/Western_Xia "Western Xia") by [Genghis Khan](/wiki/Genghis_Khan "Genghis Khan"),[[65]](#cite_note-82) who also [invaded Jin territories](/wiki/Mongol_conquest_of_the_Jin_dynasty "Mongol conquest of the Jin dynasty").[[66]](#cite_note-83) In 1271, the Mongol leader [Kublai Khan](/wiki/Kublai_Khan "Kublai Khan") established the [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty"), which [conquered the last remnant of the Song dynasty](/wiki/Mongol_conquest_of_the_Song_dynasty "Mongol conquest of the Song dynasty") in 1279. Before the Mongol invasion, the population of Song China was 120 million citizens; this was reduced to 60 million by the time of the census in 1300.[[67]](#cite_note-84) A peasant named Zhu Yuanzhang [overthrew the Yuan](/wiki/Red_Turban_Rebellions "Red Turban Rebellions") in 1368 and founded the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") as the [Hongwu Emperor](/wiki/Hongwu_Emperor "Hongwu Emperor"). Under the Ming dynasty, China enjoyed another golden age, developing one of the strongest navies in the world and a rich and prosperous economy amid a flourishing of art and culture. It was during this period that admiral [Zheng He](/wiki/Zheng_He "Zheng He") led the [Ming treasure voyages](/wiki/Ming_treasure_voyages "Ming treasure voyages") throughout the Indian Ocean, reaching as far as East Africa.[[68]](#cite_note-85)

In the early Ming dynasty, China's capital was moved from [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") to Beijing. With the budding of capitalism, philosophers such as [Wang Yangming](/wiki/Wang_Yangming "Wang Yangming") critiqued and expanded Neo-Confucianism with concepts of [individualism](/wiki/Individualism "Individualism") and equality of [four occupations](/wiki/Four_occupations "Four occupations").[[69]](#cite_note-86) The [scholar-official](/wiki/Scholar-official "Scholar-official") stratum became a supporting force of industry and commerce in the tax boycott movements, which, together with the famines and defense against [Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)](/wiki/Japanese_invasions_of_Korea_(1592%E2%80%931598) "Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)") and [Later Jin](/wiki/Later_Jin_(1616%E2%80%931636) "Later Jin (1616–1636)") incursions led to an exhausted treasury.[[70]](#cite_note-87) In 1644, Beijing was captured by a coalition of [peasant rebel](/wiki/Late_Ming_peasant_rebellions "Late Ming peasant rebellions") forces led by [Li Zicheng](/wiki/Li_Zicheng "Li Zicheng"). The [Chongzhen Emperor](/wiki/Chongzhen_Emperor "Chongzhen Emperor") committed suicide when the city fell. The Manchu [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), then allied with Ming dynasty general [Wu Sangui](/wiki/Wu_Sangui "Wu Sangui"), overthrew Li's short-lived [Shun dynasty](/wiki/Shun_dynasty "Shun dynasty") and subsequently seized control of Beijing, which became the new capital of the Qing dynasty.[[71]](#cite_note-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Qing_Empire_circa_1820_EN.svg/330px-Qing_Empire_circa_1820_EN.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Qing_Empire_circa_1820_EN.svg)

The [Qing conquest of the Ming](/wiki/Qing_conquest_of_the_Ming "Qing conquest of the Ming") and expansion of the empire

The Qing dynasty, which lasted from 1644 until 1912, was the last imperial dynasty of China. The [transition from Ming to Qing](/wiki/Transition_from_Ming_to_Qing "Transition from Ming to Qing") (1618–1683) cost 25 million lives, but the Qing appeared to have restored China's imperial power and inaugurated another flowering of the arts.[[72]](#cite_note-89) After the [Southern Ming](/wiki/Southern_Ming "Southern Ming") ended, the further conquest of the [Dzungar Khanate](/wiki/Dzungar_Khanate "Dzungar Khanate") added Mongolia, Tibet and Xinjiang to the empire.[[73]](#cite_note-90) Meanwhile, China's population growth resumed and shortly began to accelerate. It is commonly agreed that pre-modern China's population experienced two growth spurts, one during the [Northern Song](/wiki/Northern_Song "Northern Song") period (960–1127), and other during the Qing period (around 1700–1830).[[74]](#cite_note-91) By the [High Qing era](/wiki/High_Qing_era "High Qing era") China was possibly the most commercialized country in the world, and imperial China experienced a second commercial revolution by the end of the 18th century.[[75]](#cite_note-92) On the other hand, the centralized autocracy was strengthened in part to suppress [anti-Qing sentiment](/wiki/Anti-Qing_sentiment "Anti-Qing sentiment") with the policy of valuing agriculture and restraining commerce, like the *[Haijin](/wiki/Haijin "Haijin")* during the early Qing period and ideological control as represented by the [literary inquisition](/wiki/Literary_inquisition "Literary inquisition"), causing some social and technological stagnation.[[76]](#cite_note-93)[[77]](#cite_note-94)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/EightNations_in1901.jpg/250px-EightNations_in1901.jpg)](/wiki/File:EightNations_in1901.jpg)

The [Eight-Nation Alliance](/wiki/Eight-Nation_Alliance "Eight-Nation Alliance") invaded China to defeat the anti-foreign [Boxers](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion") and their Qing backers. The image shows a celebration ceremony inside the Chinese imperial palace, the [Forbidden City](/wiki/Forbidden_City "Forbidden City") after the signing of the [Boxer Protocol](/wiki/Boxer_Protocol "Boxer Protocol") in 1901.

In the mid-19th century, the [Opium Wars](/wiki/Opium_Wars "Opium Wars") with Britain and [France](/wiki/France "France") forced China to pay compensation, open treaty ports, allow [extraterritoriality](/wiki/Extraterritoriality "Extraterritoriality") for foreign nationals, and cede [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") to the British[[78]](#cite_note-95) under the 1842 [Treaty of Nanking](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanking "Treaty of Nanking"), the first of what have been termed the [unequal treaties](/wiki/Unequal_treaty "Unequal treaty"). The [First Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/First_Sino-Japanese_War "First Sino-Japanese War") (1894–1895) resulted in Qing China's loss of influence in the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula"), as well as the [cession of Taiwan](/wiki/Treaty_of_Shimonoseki "Treaty of Shimonoseki") to [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan").[[79]](#cite_note-96) The Qing dynasty also began experiencing [internal unrest](/wiki/Timeline_of_late_anti-Qing_rebellions "Timeline of late anti-Qing rebellions") in which tens of millions of people died, especially in the [White Lotus Rebellion](/wiki/White_Lotus_Rebellion "White Lotus Rebellion"), the failed [Taiping Rebellion](/wiki/Taiping_Rebellion "Taiping Rebellion") that ravaged southern China in the 1850s and 1860s and the [Dungan Revolt (1862–1877)](/wiki/Dungan_Revolt_(1862%E2%80%931877) "Dungan Revolt (1862–1877)") in the northwest. The initial success of the [Self-Strengthening Movement](/wiki/Self-Strengthening_Movement "Self-Strengthening Movement") of the 1860s was frustrated by a series of military defeats in the 1880s and 1890s.[[80]](#cite_note-Lee2004-97) In the 19th century, the great [Chinese diaspora](/wiki/Chinese_emigration "Chinese emigration") began. Losses due to emigration were added to by conflicts and catastrophes such as the [Northern Chinese Famine of 1876–1879](/wiki/Northern_Chinese_Famine_of_1876%E2%80%931879 "Northern Chinese Famine of 1876–1879"), in which between 9 and 13 million people died.[[81]](#cite_note-98) The [Guangxu Emperor](/wiki/Guangxu_Emperor "Guangxu Emperor") drafted a [reform plan](/wiki/Hundred_Days%27_Reform "Hundred Days' Reform") in 1898 to establish a modern [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), but these plans were thwarted by the [Empress Dowager Cixi](/wiki/Empress_Dowager_Cixi "Empress Dowager Cixi"). The ill-fated anti-foreign [Boxer Rebellion](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion") of 1899–1901 further weakened the dynasty. Although Cixi sponsored a program of reforms known as the [late Qing reforms](/wiki/Late_Qing_reforms "Late Qing reforms"), the [Xinhai Revolution](/wiki/1911_Revolution "1911 Revolution") of 1911 ended the Qing dynasty.[[82]](#cite_note-xb1-99) [Puyi](/wiki/Puyi "Puyi"), the last Emperor, [abdicated in 1912](/wiki/Imperial_Edict_of_the_Abdication_of_the_Qing_Emperor "Imperial Edict of the Abdication of the Qing Emperor").[[83]](#cite_note-abdicate-100)

### Republic of China

Main article: [Republic of China (1912–1949)](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)")

Further information: [1911 Revolution](/wiki/1911_Revolution "1911 Revolution"), [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War"), [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War"), and [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution")

On 1 January 1912, the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") (ROC) was established, and [Sun Yat-sen](/wiki/Sun_Yat-sen "Sun Yat-sen") of the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") was proclaimed provisional president.[[84]](#cite_note-101) In 1912, the presidency was given to [Yuan Shikai](/wiki/Yuan_Shikai "Yuan Shikai"), a former Qing general who in 1915 proclaimed himself [Emperor of China](/wiki/Empire_of_China_(1915%E2%80%931916) "Empire of China (1915–1916)"). In the face of popular condemnation and opposition from his own [Beiyang Army](/wiki/Beiyang_Army "Beiyang Army"), he was forced to re-establish the republic in 1916.[[85]](#cite_note-102) After Yuan's death in 1916, China was politically fragmented. Its Beijing-based government was internationally recognized but virtually powerless; regional warlords controlled most of its territory.[[86]](#cite_note-103)[[87]](#cite_note-104) During this [period](/wiki/Warlord_Era "Warlord Era"), China [participated in](/wiki/China_during_World_War_I "China during World War I") [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and saw a far-reaching popular uprising called the [May Fourth Movement](/wiki/May_Fourth_Movement "May Fourth Movement").[[88]](#cite_note-105) In the late 1920s, the Kuomintang under [Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek") reunified most the country through the [Northern Expedition](/wiki/Northern_Expedition "Northern Expedition").[[89]](#cite_note-106)[[90]](#cite_note-107) The Kuomintang moved the nation's capital to [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") and implemented "political tutelage", an intermediate stage of political development outlined in Sun Yat-sen's [Three Principles of the People](/wiki/Three_Principles_of_the_People "Three Principles of the People") program for transforming China into a modern democratic state.[[91]](#cite_note-108)[[92]](#cite_note-109) The Kuomintang [briefly allied](/wiki/First_United_Front "First United Front") with the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP) during the Northern Expedition, though the alliance broke down in 1927 after Chiang [violently suppressed](/wiki/Shanghai_massacre "Shanghai massacre") the CCP and other leftists in Shanghai, marking the beginning of the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War").[[93]](#cite_note-110) CCP forces in Jiangxi were [wiped out](/wiki/Encirclement_campaigns "Encirclement campaigns") by the Nationalist government in 1934, leading the CCP to initiate the [Long March](/wiki/Long_March "Long March") and relocate to [Yan'an](/wiki/Yan%27an "Yan'an") in [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi"). It would be the base of the communists before major combat in the Chinese Civil War ended in 1949.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/1945_Mao_and_Chiang.jpg/250px-1945_Mao_and_Chiang.jpg)](/wiki/File:1945_Mao_and_Chiang.jpg)

[Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek") and [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") toasting together in 1945 following the [end of World War II](/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Asia "End of World War II in Asia")

In 1931, Japan [invaded and occupied Manchuria](/wiki/Japanese_invasion_of_Manchuria "Japanese invasion of Manchuria"). Japan invaded other parts of China in 1937, precipitating the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") (1937–1945), a [theater](/wiki/Theater_(warfare) "Theater (warfare)") of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The war forced an [uneasy alliance](/wiki/Second_United_Front "Second United Front") between the Kuomintang and the CCP. Japanese forces committed numerous [war atrocities](/wiki/Japanese_war_crimes "Japanese war crimes") against the civilian population; as many as 20 million Chinese civilians died.[[94]](#cite_note-111) An estimated 40,000 to over 300,000 Chinese were [massacred in Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing_Massacre "Nanjing Massacre") alone during the Japanese occupation.[[95]](#cite_note-:142-112) The Republic of China, along with the UK, the United States, and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), were recognized as the Allied "[Big Four](/wiki/Four_Policemen "Four Policemen")" in the [Declaration by United Nations](/wiki/Declaration_by_United_Nations "Declaration by United Nations").[[96]](#cite_note-113)[[97]](#cite_note-114) Along with the other three great powers, China was one of the four major [Allies of World War II](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"), and was later considered one of the primary victors in the war.[[98]](#cite_note-115)[[99]](#cite_note-116) After the [surrender of Japan](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan") in 1945, China emerged victorious but war-ravaged and financially drained. Taiwan, along with the [Penghu](/wiki/Penghu "Penghu"), were [handed over to ROC control](/wiki/Retrocession_of_Taiwan "Retrocession of Taiwan"); however, the validity of this handover is controversial.[[100]](#cite_note-:1-117) In 1946, fighting between CCP and KMT forces broke out and the country resumed a state of civil war that lasted more than three years.[[101]](#cite_note-118) Constitutional rule was established in 1947, but because of the ongoing war, many provisions of the [ROC constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Republic_of_China "Constitution of the Republic of China") were never implemented in mainland China.[[100]](#cite_note-:1-117)

### People's Republic of China

Main article: [History of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "History of the People's Republic of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Mao_Proclaiming_New_China.JPG/250px-Mao_Proclaiming_New_China.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mao_Proclaiming_New_China.JPG)

On 1 October 1949, [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") announced the [founding of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China")

By 1949, the CCP took control of most of mainland China, and the [ROC government retreated to Taiwan](/wiki/Retreat_of_the_government_of_the_Republic_of_China_to_Taiwan "Retreat of the government of the Republic of China to Taiwan"). On 1 October 1949, [CCP Chairman](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party") [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") formally [proclaimed the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China").[[102]](#cite_note-119) In 1950, the PRC [captured Hainan](/wiki/Battle_of_Hainan_Island "Battle of Hainan Island") from the ROC[[103]](#cite_note-120) and [annexed Tibet](/wiki/Annexation_of_Tibet_by_China "Annexation of Tibet by China").[[104]](#cite_note-121) The CCP consolidated its popularity among the peasants through the [Land Reform Movement](/wiki/Land_Reform_Movement "Land Reform Movement"), which included the state-tolerated executions of between 1 and 2 million landlords by peasants and former tenants.[[105]](#cite_note-122) Though the PRC initially allied closely with the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), the relations between the two [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism") nations [gradually deteriorated](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split"), leading China to develop an independent industrial system and [its own nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_China "Nuclear weapons of China").[[106]](#cite_note-123) The Chinese population increased from 550 million in 1950 to 900 million in 1974.[[107]](#cite_note-124) However, the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward"), a massive [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") project, resulted in [an estimated 15 to 55 million deaths](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine") between 1959 and 1961, mostly from starvation.[[108]](#cite_note-nyt-125)[[109]](#cite_note-126) In 1964, China [detonated its first atomic bomb](/wiki/Project_596 "Project 596").[[110]](#cite_note-127) In 1966, Mao and his allies launched the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), sparking a decade of political recrimination and social upheaval that lasted until Mao's [death](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Mao_Zedong "Death and state funeral of Mao Zedong") in 1976.[[111]](#cite_note-128)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Ud%C3%A1losti_na_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Tian_an_men%2C_%C4%8C%C3%ADna_1989%2C_foto_Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Tondl.jpg/250px-Ud%C3%A1losti_na_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Tian_an_men%2C_%C4%8C%C3%ADna_1989%2C_foto_Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Tondl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ud%C3%A1losti_na_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Tian_an_men,_%C4%8C%C3%ADna_1989,_foto_Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Tondl.jpg)

The [1989 Tiananmen Square protests](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests "1989 Tiananmen Square protests") were ended by a military-led massacre.

After Mao's death, the [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four") were [arrested](/wiki/Smashing_the_Gang_of_Four "Smashing the Gang of Four") by [Hua Guofeng](/wiki/Hua_Guofeng "Hua Guofeng"). The Cultural Revolution was rebuked, with millions rehabilitated. [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") took power in 1978, and started the [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up"), instituting large-scale [political](/wiki/Boluan_Fanzheng "Boluan Fanzheng") and economic reforms to transition away from planned economy.[[112]](#cite_note-Ref_e-129)[[113]](#cite_note-Hamrin-1995-130) China adopted its current [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_China "Constitution of China") on 4 December 1982.[[114]](#cite_note-131) In 1989, there were protests such [those in Tiananmen Square](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests_and_massacre "1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre"), and then throughout the entire nation.[[115]](#cite_note-132) [Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin") was elevated to become the CCP general secretary, becoming the paramount leader.[[116]](#cite_note-133)[[117]](#cite_note-134) China's economy grew sevenfold during Jiang's tenure.[[118]](#cite_note-APs-2022-135) [British Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") and [Portuguese Macau](/wiki/Portuguese_Macau "Portuguese Macau") returned to China in [1997](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") and [1999](/wiki/Handover_of_Macau "Handover of Macau"), respectively, as [special administrative regions](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_China "Special administrative regions of China") under the principle of [one country, two systems](/wiki/One_country,_two_systems "One country, two systems"). In 2002, [Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao") succeeded Jiang as the general secretary.[[118]](#cite_note-APs-2022-135) Under Hu, China maintained its high rate of economic growth, overtaking the United Kingdom, France, Germany and Japan to become the world's second-largest economy.[[119]](#cite_note-136) However, the growth also severely impacted the country's resources and environment,[[120]](#cite_note-137)[[121]](#cite_note-138) and caused major social displacement.[[122]](#cite_note-Ref_k-139)[[123]](#cite_note-140) [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") succeeded Hu as paramount leader in 2012. Shortly after his ascension to power, Xi launched [a vast anti-corruption crackdown](/wiki/Anti-corruption_campaign_under_Xi_Jinping "Anti-corruption campaign under Xi Jinping"),[[124]](#cite_note-141) that prosecuted more than 2 million officials by 2022.[[125]](#cite_note-Marquis-2022b-142) During [his tenure](/wiki/China_under_Xi_Jinping "China under Xi Jinping"), Xi has consolidated power unseen since the initiation of economic and political reforms.[[126]](#cite_note-143)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of China](/wiki/Geography_of_China "Geography of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/East_Asia_topographic_map.png/250px-East_Asia_topographic_map.png)](/wiki/File:East_Asia_topographic_map.png)

A topographic map of China

China's landscape is vast and diverse, ranging from the [Gobi](/wiki/Gobi_Desert "Gobi Desert") and [Taklamakan Deserts](/wiki/Taklamakan_Desert "Taklamakan Desert") in the arid north to the [subtropical](/wiki/Subtropical "Subtropical") forests in the wetter south. The [Himalaya](/wiki/Himalaya "Himalaya"), [Karakoram](/wiki/Karakoram "Karakoram"), [Pamir](/wiki/Pamir_Mountains "Pamir Mountains") and [Tian Shan](/wiki/Tian_Shan "Tian Shan") mountain ranges separate China from much of [South](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") and [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"). The [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River") and Yellow Rivers, the third- and sixth-longest in the world, respectively, run from the [Tibetan Plateau](/wiki/Tibetan_Plateau "Tibetan Plateau") to the densely populated eastern seaboard. China's coastline along the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") is 14,500 km (9,000 mi) long and is bounded by the [Bohai](/wiki/Bohai_Sea "Bohai Sea"), [Yellow](/wiki/Yellow_Sea "Yellow Sea"), [East China](/wiki/East_China_Sea "East China Sea") and [South China](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea") seas. China connects through the Kazakh border to the [Eurasian Steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_Steppe "Eurasian Steppe").

The territory of China lies between [latitudes](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") [18°](/wiki/18th_parallel_north "18th parallel north") and [54° N](/wiki/54th_parallel_north "54th parallel north"), and [longitudes](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") [73°](/wiki/73rd_meridian_east "73rd meridian east") and [135° E](/wiki/135th_meridian_east "135th meridian east"). The [geographical center](/wiki/Geographical_center "Geographical center") of China is marked by the Center of the Country Monument at [35°50′40.9″N 103°27′7.5″E﻿ / ﻿35.844694°N 103.452083°E﻿ / 35.844694; 103.452083﻿ (Geographical center of China)](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=China&params=35_50_40.9_N_103_27_7.5_E_region:CN-62_type:landmark&title=Geographical+center+of+China). China's landscapes vary significantly across its vast territory. In the east, along the shores of the Yellow Sea and the East China Sea, there are extensive and densely populated [alluvial plains](/wiki/Alluvial_plain "Alluvial plain"), while on the edges of the Inner Mongolian plateau in the north, broad [grasslands](/wiki/Grassland "Grassland") predominate. Southern China is dominated by hills and low mountain ranges, while the central-east hosts the [deltas](/wiki/River_delta "River delta") of China's two major rivers, the Yellow River and the Yangtze River. Other major rivers include the [Xi](/wiki/Xi_River "Xi River"), [Mekong](/wiki/Mekong "Mekong"), [Brahmaputra](/wiki/Brahmaputra_River "Brahmaputra River") and [Amur](/wiki/Amur_River "Amur River"). To the west sit major mountain ranges, most notably the Himalayas. High [plateaus](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") feature among the more arid landscapes of the north, such as the Taklamakan and the Gobi Desert. The world's highest point, [Mount Everest](/wiki/Mount_Everest "Mount Everest") (8,848 m), lies on the Sino-Nepalese border.[[127]](#cite_note-144) The country's lowest point, and the world's third-lowest, is the dried lake bed of [Ayding Lake](/wiki/Ayding_Lake "Ayding Lake") (−154 m) in the [Turpan Depression](/wiki/Turpan_Depression "Turpan Depression").[[128]](#cite_note-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Huangshan_pic_4.jpg/250px-Huangshan_pic_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huangshan_pic_4.jpg)

[Huangshan](/wiki/Huangshan "Huangshan") in [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui")

Despite spanning the equivalent of five geographical time zones (from UTC+5 to UTC+9), China uses a [single national time zone](/wiki/Time_in_China "Time in China"), China Standard Time (UTC+8).[[129]](#cite_note-whyist-146)[[130]](#cite_note-147) This uniform time policy was adopted in 1949.[[129]](#cite_note-whyist-146)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of China](/wiki/Climate_of_China "Climate of China")

Further information: [Great Green Wall (China)](/wiki/Great_Green_Wall_(China) "Great Green Wall (China)")

China's climate is mainly dominated by [dry seasons](/wiki/Dry_season "Dry season") and wet [monsoons](/wiki/Monsoons "Monsoons"), which lead to pronounced temperature differences between winter and summer. In the winter, northern winds coming from high-latitude areas are cold and dry; in summer, southern winds from coastal areas at lower latitudes are warm and moist.[[131]](#cite_note-148)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Koppen-Geiger_Map_CHN_present.svg/500px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_CHN_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_CHN_present.svg)

[Köppen-Geiger climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map for mainland China[[132]](#cite_note-149)

A major environmental issue in China is the continued [expansion of its deserts](/wiki/Desertification "Desertification"), particularly the Gobi Desert.[[133]](#cite_note-150) Although barrier tree lines planted since the 1970s have reduced the frequency of [sandstorms](/wiki/Sandstorms "Sandstorms"), prolonged drought and poor agricultural practices have resulted in [dust storms](/wiki/Asian_dust "Asian dust") plaguing northern China each spring, which then spread to other parts of East Asia, including Japan and Korea. Water quality, [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"), and [pollution control](/wiki/Pollution_in_China "Pollution in China") have become important issues in China's relations with other countries. Melting [glaciers](/wiki/Glaciers "Glaciers") in the Himalayas could potentially lead to [water shortages](/wiki/Water_shortage "Water shortage") for hundreds of millions of people.[[134]](#cite_note-msnbc-151) According to academics, in order to limit [climate change in China](/wiki/Climate_change_in_China "Climate change in China") to 1.5 °C (2.7 °F) electricity generation from [coal in China](/wiki/Coal_in_China "Coal in China") without [carbon capture](/wiki/Carbon_capture_and_storage "Carbon capture and storage") must be phased out by 2045.[[135]](#cite_note-152)

Official government statistics about Chinese agricultural productivity are considered unreliable, due to exaggeration of production at subsidiary government levels.[[136]](#cite_note-153)[[137]](#cite_note-154) Much of China has a climate very suitable for agriculture and the country has been the world's largest producer of rice, wheat, tomatoes, eggplant, grapes, watermelon, spinach, and many other crops.[[138]](#cite_note-155) In 2021, 12 percent of global permanent meadows and pastures belonged to China, as well as 8% of global cropland.[[139]](#cite_note-:14-156)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Wildlife of China](/wiki/Wildlife_of_China "Wildlife of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Giant_Panda_Eating.jpg/250px-Giant_Panda_Eating.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giant_Panda_Eating.jpg)

A [giant panda](/wiki/Giant_panda "Giant panda"), one of China's most famous symbols, at the [Chengdu Panda Base](/wiki/Chengdu_Research_Base_of_Giant_Panda_Breeding "Chengdu Research Base of Giant Panda Breeding") in [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan")

China is one of 17 [megadiverse countries](/wiki/Megadiverse_countries "Megadiverse countries"),[[140]](#cite_note-Ref_2009a-157) lying in two of the world's major [biogeographic realms](/wiki/Biogeographic_realm "Biogeographic realm"): the [Palearctic](/wiki/Palearctic_realm "Palearctic realm") and the [Indomalayan](/wiki/Indomalayan_realm "Indomalayan realm"). By one measure, China has over 34,687 species of animals and vascular plants, making it the third-most biodiverse country in the world, after [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia").[[141]](#cite_note-158) The country is a party to the [Convention on Biological Diversity](/wiki/Convention_on_Biological_Diversity "Convention on Biological Diversity");[[142]](#cite_note-159) its [National Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan](/wiki/Biodiversity_action_plan "Biodiversity action plan") was received by the convention in 2010.[[143]](#cite_note-160)

China is home to at least 551 species of [mammals](/wiki/List_of_mammals_of_China "List of mammals of China") (the third-highest in the world),[[144]](#cite_note-161) 1,221 species of birds (eighth),[[145]](#cite_note-162) 424 species of reptiles (seventh)[[146]](#cite_note-163) and 333 species of amphibians (seventh).[[147]](#cite_note-164) Wildlife in China shares habitat with, and bears acute pressure from, one of the world's largest population of humans. At least 840 [animal species are threatened, vulnerable or in danger of local extinction](/wiki/List_of_endangered_and_protected_species_of_China "List of endangered and protected species of China"), due mainly to human activity such as habitat destruction, pollution and poaching for food, fur and [traditional Chinese medicine](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_medicine "Traditional Chinese medicine").[[148]](#cite_note-165) Endangered wildlife is protected by law, and as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), the country has over 2,750 [nature reserves](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_China "Protected areas of China"), covering a total area of 1,470,000 km2 (570,000 sq mi), 15 percent of China's total land area.[[149]](#cite_note-166) Most wild animals have been eliminated from the core agricultural regions of east and central China, but they have fared better in the mountainous south and west.[[150]](#cite_note-167)[[151]](#cite_note-168) The [Baiji](/wiki/Baiji "Baiji") was confirmed extinct on 12 December 2006.[[152]](#cite_note-169)

China has over 32,000 species of vascular plants,[[153]](#cite_note-170) and is home to a variety of forest types. Cold [coniferous](/wiki/Coniferous "Coniferous") forests predominate in the north of the country, supporting animal species such as [moose](/wiki/Moose "Moose") and [Asian black bear](/wiki/Asian_black_bear "Asian black bear"), along with over 120 bird species.[[154]](#cite_note-rough_guide-171) The [understory](/wiki/Understory "Understory") of moist [conifer](/wiki/Conifer "Conifer") forests may contain thickets of [bamboo](/wiki/Bamboo "Bamboo"). In higher [montane](/wiki/Montane_ecosystems "Montane ecosystems") stands of [juniper](/wiki/Juniper "Juniper") and [yew](/wiki/Taxus "Taxus"), the bamboo is replaced by [rhododendrons](/wiki/Rhododendron "Rhododendron"). [Subtropical](/wiki/Subtropical "Subtropical") forests, which are predominate in central and southern China, support a high density of plant species including numerous rare endemics. Tropical and seasonal [rainforests](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest"), though confined to [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") and [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan"), contain a quarter of all the animal and plant species found in China.[[154]](#cite_note-rough_guide-171) China has over 10,000 recorded species of [fungi](/wiki/Fungi "Fungi").[[155]](#cite_note-172)

### Environment

Main articles: [Environment of China](/wiki/Environment_of_China "Environment of China") and [Environmental issues in China](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_China "Environmental issues in China")

See also: [Renewable energy in China](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_China "Renewable energy in China"), [Water resources of China](/wiki/Water_resources_of_China "Water resources of China"), [Energy policy of China](/wiki/Energy_policy_of_China "Energy policy of China"), and [Climate change in China](/wiki/Climate_change_in_China "Climate change in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/ThreeGorgesDam-China2009.jpg/250px-ThreeGorgesDam-China2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:ThreeGorgesDam-China2009.jpg)

The [Three Gorges Dam](/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam "Three Gorges Dam") is the largest hydroelectric dam in the world.

In the early 2000s, China has suffered from [environmental deterioration and pollution](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_China "Environmental issues in China") due to its rapid pace of industrialization.[[156]](#cite_note-Ma2002-173)[[157]](#cite_note-174) Environmental regulations fairly stringent, though they are poorly enforced, frequently disregarded in favor of rapid economic development.[[158]](#cite_note-175) China has the second-highest death toll because of air pollution, after [India](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_India "Environmental issues in India"), with approximately 1 million deaths.[[159]](#cite_note-176)[[160]](#cite_note-177) Although China ranks as the highest [CO2 emitting](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_carbon_dioxide_emissions "List of countries by carbon dioxide emissions") country, it only emits 8 tons of [CO2 per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_carbon_dioxide_emissions_per_capita "List of countries by carbon dioxide emissions per capita"), significantly lower than developed countries such as the United States (16.1), Australia (16.8) and South Korea (13.6).[[161]](#cite_note-UCS-2020-178) [Greenhouse gas emissions by China](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions_by_China "Greenhouse gas emissions by China") are the [world's largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_greenhouse_gas_emissions "List of countries by greenhouse gas emissions").[[161]](#cite_note-UCS-2020-178) 90.4% of China's national surface water was graded suitable for human consumption by the [Ministry of Ecology and Environment](/wiki/Ministry_of_Ecology_and_Environment "Ministry of Ecology and Environment") in 2024.[[162]](#cite_note-179)

China has prioritized clamping down on pollution, bringing a significant decrease in air pollution in the 2010s.[[163]](#cite_note-180) In 2020, the Chinese government announced its aims for the country to reach its peak emissions levels before 2030, and achieve carbon neutrality by 2060 in line with the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement"), which, according to [Climate Action Tracker](/wiki/Climate_Action_Tracker "Climate Action Tracker"), would lower the expected rise in global temperature by 0.2–0.3 degrees – "the biggest single reduction ever estimated by the Climate Action Tracker".[[164]](#cite_note-CAT-2020-181) According to China's government, the forest coverage of the country grew from 10% of the overall territory in 1949 to 25% in 2024.[[165]](#cite_note-182)

China is the world's leading investor in [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") and [its commercialization](/wiki/Renewable_energy_commercialization "Renewable energy commercialization"), with [$](/wiki/US$ "US$")546 billion invested in 2022;[[166]](#cite_note-Schonhardt-2023-183) it the world's leading manufacturer and innovator of renewable energy technologies and invests heavily in local-scale renewable energy projects.[[167]](#cite_note-184)[[166]](#cite_note-Schonhardt-2023-183)[[168]](#cite_note-:37-185) Long heavily relying on non-renewable energy sources such as coal, China's adaptation of [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_China "Renewable energy in China") has increased significantly in recent years.[[169]](#cite_note-186) In 2024, 58.2% of China's electricity came from [coal](/wiki/Coal_in_China "Coal in China") (largest producer in the world), 13.5% from [hydroelectric power](/wiki/Hydroelectric_power_in_Himachal_Pradesh "Hydroelectric power in Himachal Pradesh") (largest), 9.8% from [wind](/wiki/Wind_power_in_China "Wind power in China") (largest), 8.3% from [solar energy](/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power") (largest), 4.4% from [nuclear energy](/wiki/Nuclear_power_in_China "Nuclear power in China") (second-largest), 3% from [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas_in_China "Natural gas in China") (fifth-largest), and 2.1% from [bioenergy](/wiki/Bioenergy "Bioenergy") (largest); in total, 38% of China's energy came from clean energy sources.[[170]](#cite_note-187) Despite its emphasis on renewables, China remains deeply connected to global oil markets and next to India, has been the largest importer of Russian [crude oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") in 2022.[[171]](#cite_note-188)[[172]](#cite_note-189)

### Political geography

Main articles: [Borders of China](/wiki/Borders_of_China "Borders of China"), [Coastline of China](/wiki/Coastline_of_China "Coastline of China"), and [Territorial changes of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Territorial_changes_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Territorial changes of the People's Republic of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/China_administrative.png/250px-China_administrative.png)](/wiki/File:China_administrative.png)

Map depicting territorial disputes between the PRC and neighboring states.

China is the [second-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area") country by land area after [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), and the third- or fourth-largest country by total area.[[r]](#cite_note-190) China's total area is generally stated as being approximately 9,600,000 km2 (3,700,000 sq mi).[[173]](#cite_note-191) Specific area figures range from 9,572,900 km2 (3,696,100 sq mi) according to the *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")*,[[12]](#cite_note-United_States-22) to 9,596,961 km2 (3,705,407 sq mi) according to the *[UN Demographic Yearbook](/wiki/Yearbook_of_the_United_Nations "Yearbook of the United Nations")*,[[5]](#cite_note-UN_Stat-8) and *[The World Factbook](/wiki/The_World_Factbook "The World Factbook")*.[[4]](#cite_note-CIA-7)

China has the [longest combined land border in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_by_number_of_land_borders "List of countries and territories by number of land borders"), measuring 22,117 km (13,743 mi) and its [coastline](/wiki/Coastline_of_China "Coastline of China") covers approximately 14,500 km (9,000 mi) from the mouth of the [Yalu River](/wiki/Yalu_River "Yalu River") (Amnok River) to the [Gulf of Tonkin](/wiki/Gulf_of_Tonkin "Gulf of Tonkin").[[4]](#cite_note-CIA-7) China [borders 14 nations](/wiki/Borders_of_China "Borders of China") and covers the bulk of East Asia, bordering [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), and [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") in Southeast Asia; [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")[[s]](#cite_note-192) and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") in South Asia; [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan"), [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan") and [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan") in Central Asia; and [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"), and [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") in [Inner Asia](/wiki/Inner_Asia "Inner Asia") and [Northeast Asia](/wiki/Northeast_Asia "Northeast Asia"). It is narrowly separated from [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") and [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") to the southwest and south, and has several maritime neighbors such as [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").[[174]](#cite_note-193)

China has resolved its land borders with 12 out of 14 neighboring countries, having pursued substantial compromises in most of them.[[175]](#cite_note-194)[[176]](#cite_note-195)[[177]](#cite_note-196) China currently has a disputed land border with [India](/wiki/Sino-Indian_border_dispute "Sino-Indian border dispute")[[178]](#cite_note-197) and [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan%E2%80%93China_border "Bhutan–China border").[[179]](#cite_note-198) China is additionally involved in maritime disputes with multiple countries over territory in the [East](/wiki/East_China_Sea_EEZ_disputes "East China Sea EEZ disputes") and [South China Seas](/wiki/Territorial_disputes_in_the_South_China_Sea "Territorial disputes in the South China Sea"), such as the [Senkaku Islands](/wiki/Senkaku_Islands_dispute "Senkaku Islands dispute"), which it refers as the Diaoyu Islands, and the entirety of [South China Sea Islands](/wiki/South_China_Sea_Islands "South China Sea Islands").[[180]](#cite_note-199)[[181]](#cite_note-200)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of China](/wiki/Politics_of_China "Politics of China")

See also: [Party and state leaders](/wiki/Party_and_state_leaders "Party and state leaders") and [List of current Chinese provincial leaders](/wiki/List_of_current_Chinese_provincial_leaders "List of current Chinese provincial leaders")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/China_Senate_House.jpg/250px-China_Senate_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:China_Senate_House.jpg)

The [Great Hall of the People](/wiki/Great_Hall_of_the_People "Great Hall of the People")  
where the [National People's Congress](/wiki/National_People%27s_Congress "National People's Congress") convenes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/%E4%B8%AD%E5%8D%97%E6%B5%B7_%E5%82%8D%E6%99%9A_%E4%B8%9C%E5%8D%97%E4%BE%A7.jpg/250px-%E4%B8%AD%E5%8D%97%E6%B5%B7_%E5%82%8D%E6%99%9A_%E4%B8%9C%E5%8D%97%E4%BE%A7.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E4%B8%AD%E5%8D%97%E6%B5%B7_%E5%82%8D%E6%99%9A_%E4%B8%9C%E5%8D%97%E4%BE%A7.jpg)

The [Zhongnanhai](/wiki/Zhongnanhai "Zhongnanhai"), headquarters of the [Chinese government](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "State Council of the People's Republic of China") and [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party")

The People's Republic of China is a [communist state](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state") under the [absolute leadership](/wiki/The_Party_leads_everything "The Party leads everything") of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP). The CCP is officially [guided](/wiki/Ideology_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Ideology of the Chinese Communist Party") by [socialism with Chinese characteristics](/wiki/Socialism_with_Chinese_characteristics "Socialism with Chinese characteristics"), which it describes as [Marxism adapted to Chinese circumstances](/wiki/Chinese_Marxist_philosophy "Chinese Marxist philosophy").[[182]](#cite_note-201) The [Chinese constitution](/wiki/Chinese_constitution "Chinese constitution") states that the PRC "is a [socialist state](/wiki/Socialist_state_(communism) "Socialist state (communism)") governed by a [people's democratic dictatorship](/wiki/People%27s_democratic_dictatorship "People's democratic dictatorship") that is led by the working class and based on an alliance of workers and peasants", and that "the defining feature of socialism with Chinese characteristics is the leadership of the Chinese Communist Party."[[183]](#cite_note-2018-amendments-translated2-202)

The PRC officially characterizes itself as [a democracy](/wiki/Democracy_in_China "Democracy in China")—more specifically, a [whole-process people's democracy](/wiki/Whole-process_people%27s_democracy "Whole-process people's democracy")—that is organized around the [Leninist](/wiki/Leninist "Leninist") principle of [democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism").[[184]](#cite_note-Decoding_China-2021-203) However, the country is commonly described as an [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") one-party state and a [dictatorship](/wiki/Dictatorship "Dictatorship"),[[185]](#cite_note-204)[[186]](#cite_note-Isabelle-205) with some of the world's heaviest restrictions in many civil areas, most notably against [freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press_in_China "Freedom of the press in China"), [freedom of assembly](/wiki/Freedom_of_assembly "Freedom of assembly"), [free formation of social organizations](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization"), [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_China "Freedom of religion in China") and [freedom of the Internet](/wiki/Internet_censorship_in_China "Internet censorship in China").[[187]](#cite_note-freedomhouse-206) China has consistently been ranked amongst the lowest as an "authoritarian regime" by the [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit")'s [Democracy Index](/wiki/The_Economist_Democracy_Index "The Economist Democracy Index"), ranking at 145th out of 167 countries in 2024.[[188]](#cite_note-207) In the [V-Dem Democracy Indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_Indices "V-Dem Democracy Indices"), China ranks amongst the lowest as a "closed autocracy", ranking at 175th and 172nd out of 179 countries in electoral democracy and liberal democracy indices respectively.[[189]](#cite_note-j496-208) Other sources suggest that terming China as "authoritarian" does not sufficiently account for the multiple consultation mechanisms that exist in the Chinese governmental system.[[190]](#cite_note-209)

### Chinese Communist Party

Main article: [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/%E5%BA%86%E7%A5%9D%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E5%85%B1%E4%BA%A7%E5%85%9A%E6%88%90%E7%AB%8B100%E5%91%A8%E5%B9%B4%E5%A4%A7%E4%BC%9A_%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%89%E9%97%A8%E5%B9%BF%E5%9C%BA_%E5%B8%83%E6%99%AF.jpg/250px-%E5%BA%86%E7%A5%9D%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E5%85%B1%E4%BA%A7%E5%85%9A%E6%88%90%E7%AB%8B100%E5%91%A8%E5%B9%B4%E5%A4%A7%E4%BC%9A_%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%89%E9%97%A8%E5%B9%BF%E5%9C%BA_%E5%B8%83%E6%99%AF.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%BA%86%E7%A5%9D%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E5%85%B1%E4%BA%A7%E5%85%9A%E6%88%90%E7%AB%8B100%E5%91%A8%E5%B9%B4%E5%A4%A7%E4%BC%9A_%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%89%E9%97%A8%E5%B9%BF%E5%9C%BA_%E5%B8%83%E6%99%AF.jpg)

The [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") is the founding and governing political party of the People's Republic of China.

The CCP is the founding and sole ruling party of the PRC. According to the [CCP constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Constitution of the Chinese Communist Party"), the Party's highest body is the [National Congress](/wiki/National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") held every five years.[[191]](#cite_note-Ruwitch-2022-210) The National Congress elects the [Central Committee](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party"), which convenes at least once a year. The Central Committee then elects the party's [Politburo](/wiki/Politburo_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party"), [Politburo Standing Committee](/wiki/Politburo_Standing_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo Standing Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") and the [general secretary](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") ([party leader](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Leader of the Chinese Communist Party")), the top leadership of the country.[[191]](#cite_note-Ruwitch-2022-210) The general secretary holds ultimate power and authority over party and state and serves as the [paramount leader](/wiki/Paramount_leader "Paramount leader") of China.[[192]](#cite_note-211) The current general secretary is [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping"), who took office on 15 November 2012.[[193]](#cite_note-PhillipsGuardian2-212) The National Congress also elects the [Central Commission for Discipline Inspection](/wiki/Central_Commission_for_Discipline_Inspection "Central Commission for Discipline Inspection"), the CCP's supreme disciplinary organ.[[194]](#cite_note-213)

The government in China is under the sole control of the CCP.[[195]](#cite_note-:12-214) The CCP controls appointments in government bodies, with most senior government officials being CCP members.[[195]](#cite_note-:12-214) The CCP maintains committees in the each level of government. At the local level, the CCP committee of a subdivision and its [secretary](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party_Committee_Secretary "Chinese Communist Party Committee Secretary") outranks the local government and its head; CCP committee of a provincial division and its secretary outranks the provincial people's government and the governor while the CCP committee of a city and its secretary outranks the municipal people's government the mayor.[[196]](#cite_note-215)
