The [National People's Congress](/wiki/National_People%27s_Congress "National People's Congress") (NPC), with nearly 3,000-members, as the [supreme organ of state power](/wiki/Supreme_state_organ_of_power "Supreme state organ of power") holds the [unified powers of the state](/wiki/Unified_state_power "Unified state power"),[[197]](#cite_note-Constitution-216) creating a system where all state organs including the [presidency](/wiki/President_of_China "President of China"), the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_China "State Council of China"), the [State Central Military Commission](/wiki/Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Central Military Commission (China)"), the [Supreme People's Court](/wiki/Supreme_People%27s_Court "Supreme People's Court"), the [Supreme People's Procuratorate](/wiki/Supreme_People%27s_Procuratorate "Supreme People's Procuratorate"), and the [National Supervisory Commission](/wiki/National_Supervisory_Commission "National Supervisory Commission") are subject to its oversight through democratic centralism. However, observers often describe it as a "[rubber stamp](/wiki/Rubber_stamp_(politics) "Rubber stamp (politics)")" body.[[198]](#cite_note-BBC_News-2009-217) The NPC meets annually, while the [NPC Standing Committee](/wiki/Standing_Committee_of_the_National_People%27s_Congress "Standing Committee of the National People's Congress"), around 175 members elected from NPC delegates, meets every couple of months.[[198]](#cite_note-BBC_News-2009-217) [Elections](/wiki/Elections_in_China "Elections in China") are indirect and not pluralistic, with nominations at all levels being controlled by the CCP by law.[[184]](#cite_note-Decoding_China-2021-203)[[199]](#cite_note-:62-218) The NPC is dominated by the CCP, with another [eight minor parties](/wiki/Democratic_parties_(China) "Democratic parties (China)") having nominal representation under the condition of upholding CCP leadership.[[200]](#cite_note-HRW-2021-219)

The NPC elects the president, who is the ceremonial state representative. The incumbent president is [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping"), who is also the general secretary of the CCP and the [chairman of the Central Military Commission](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Chairman of the Central Military Commission (China)"), making him China's paramount leader and [supreme commander](/wiki/Supreme_command_of_the_armed_forces_in_China "Supreme command of the armed forces in China") of the armed forces. The [premier](/wiki/Premier_of_China "Premier of China") is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), with [Li Qiang](/wiki/Li_Qiang "Li Qiang") being the incumbent. The premier is officially nominated by the president and then elected by the NPC, and has generally been either the second- or third-ranking member of the Politburo Standing Committee (PSC). The premier presides over the State Council, China's cabinet, composed of four vice premiers, [state councillors](/wiki/State_councillor "State councillor"), and the heads of ministries and commissions.[[183]](#cite_note-2018-amendments-translated2-202) The [Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference](/wiki/Chinese_People%27s_Political_Consultative_Conference "Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference") (CPPCC) is a political advisory body that is critical in China's "[united front](/wiki/United_front_(China) "United front (China)")" system, which aims to gather non-CCP voices to support the CCP. Similar to the people's congresses, CPPCCs have subdivisions; the [National Committee of the CPPCC](/wiki/National_Committee_of_the_Chinese_People%27s_Political_Consultative_Conference "National Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference") is chaired by [Wang Huning](/wiki/Wang_Huning "Wang Huning"), the fourth-ranking member of the PSC.[[201]](#cite_note-220)

The governance of China is characterized by a high degree of political centralization but significant economic decentralization.[[202]](#cite_note-:0-221): 7 Policy instruments or processes are often tested locally before being applied more widely, resulting in a policy that involves experimentation and feedback.[[203]](#cite_note-222): 14 Generally, central government leadership refrains from drafting specific policies, instead using the informal networks and site visits to affirm or suggest changes to the direction of local policy experiments or pilot programs.[[204]](#cite_note-:44-223): 71 The typical approach is that central government leadership begins drafting formal policies, law, or regulations after policy has been developed at local levels.[[204]](#cite_note-:44-223): 71

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Administrative divisions of China](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_China "Administrative divisions of China"), [Districts of Hong Kong](/wiki/Districts_of_Hong_Kong "Districts of Hong Kong"), and [Municipalities and parishes of Macau](/wiki/Municipalities_and_parishes_of_Macau "Municipalities and parishes of Macau")

The PRC is constitutionally a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") divided into 23 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_China "Provinces of China"),[[t]](#cite_note-TaiwanClaim-224) five [autonomous regions](/wiki/Autonomous_regions_of_China "Autonomous regions of China") (each with a designated minority group), four [direct-administered municipalities](/wiki/Direct-administered_municipality "Direct-administered municipality")—collectively referred to as "mainland China"—as well as the [special administrative regions](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_China "Special administrative regions of China") (SARs) of Hong Kong and Macau.[[205]](#cite_note-225) The PRC regards the [island of Taiwan](/wiki/Geography_of_Taiwan "Geography of Taiwan") as its [Taiwan Province](/wiki/Taiwan_Province,_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Taiwan Province, People's Republic of China"), [Kinmen](/wiki/Kinmen "Kinmen") and [Matsu](/wiki/Matsu_Islands "Matsu Islands") as a part of [Fujian Province](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"), and islands the ROC controls in the South China Sea as a part of [Hainan Province](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan") and [Guangdong Province](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), though all these territories are governed by the [Republic of China](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") (ROC).[[206]](#cite_note-226)[[30]](#cite_note-asia-34729538-46) Geographically, all 31 provincial divisions of mainland China can be grouped into six regions: [North China](/wiki/North_China "North China"), [East China](/wiki/East_China "East China"), [Southwestern China](/wiki/Southwestern_China "Southwestern China"), [South Central China](/wiki/South_Central_China "South Central China"), [Northeast China](/wiki/Northeast_China "Northeast China"), and [Northwestern China](/wiki/Northwestern_China "Northwestern China").[[207]](#cite_note-Brown2013-227)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of China](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_China "Foreign relations of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Diplomatic_relations_of_China.svg/500px-Diplomatic_relations_of_China.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Diplomatic_relations_of_China.svg)

Diplomatic relations of China

The PRC has diplomatic relations with 179 [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") member-states and maintains [embassies in 174](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_China "List of diplomatic missions of China"). As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), China has the largest diplomatic networks of any country in the world.[[208]](#cite_note-228) China is of the five permanent members of the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council").[[209]](#cite_note-Ref_r-229) It is a member of intergovernmental organizations including the [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20"),[[210]](#cite_note-230) the [Shanghai Cooperation Organisation](/wiki/Shanghai_Cooperation_Organisation "Shanghai Cooperation Organisation"),[[211]](#cite_note-231) the [BRICS](/wiki/BRICS "BRICS"),[[212]](#cite_note-232) the [East Asia Summit](/wiki/East_Asia_Summit "East Asia Summit"),[[213]](#cite_note-233) and the [APEC](/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation").[[214]](#cite_note-234) China is also a former member and leader of the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement") and still considers itself an advocate for [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_countries "Developing countries").[[215]](#cite_note-Ref_2009-235) China is widely [described as either a potential or established superpower](/wiki/China_as_a_potential_superpower "China as a potential superpower"),[[216]](#cite_note-236)[[217]](#cite_note-237)[[218]](#cite_note-238) due to its influence in the fields of geopolitics, technology, manufacturing, economics and culture.[[219]](#cite_note-239)[[220]](#cite_note-240)[[221]](#cite_note-241)

Much of current [Chinese foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_China "Foreign policy of China") is officially based on Premier [Zhou Enlai](/wiki/Zhou_Enlai "Zhou Enlai")'s [Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence](/wiki/Five_Principles_of_Peaceful_Coexistence "Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence"), as well as by the concept of "harmony without uniformity", which encourages diplomatic relations between states despite ideological differences.[[222]](#cite_note-Keith-242) Per its policy of non-alignment, China maintains no military alliances except its [defense treaty](/wiki/Treaty_on_Friendship,_Cooperation_and_Mutual_Assistance_between_China_and_the_DPRK "Treaty on Friendship, Cooperation and Mutual Assistance between China and the DPRK") with [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea").[[223]](#cite_note-243) Most countries have switched recognition from the ROC to the PRC since the latter replaced the former in the UN in 1971.[[224]](#cite_note-244) The PRC officially maintains the [one China principle](/wiki/One_China_principle "One China principle"): the view that there is only one sovereign state with the name "China"—represented by the PRC—and that Taiwan is an integral part of China. The unique status of Taiwan has led to countries formally recognizing the PRC to maintain unique "[one China](/wiki/One_China "One China") policies" that differ from each other; some countries [explicitly recognize](/wiki/International_recognition_of_Taiwan "International recognition of Taiwan") the PRC's claim over Taiwan, while others, including the U.S. and Japan, only *acknowledge* the claim.[[225]](#cite_note-Drun-2017-245)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/16th_BRICS_Summit_family_photograph_%282024%29.jpg/250px-16th_BRICS_Summit_family_photograph_%282024%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:16th_BRICS_Summit_family_photograph_(2024).jpg)

Chinese President [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") at the [16th BRICS summit](/wiki/16th_BRICS_summit "16th BRICS summit") in [Kazan](/wiki/Kazan "Kazan"), Russia, 23 October 2024

China has a [close political, economic and military relationship](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Russia_relations "China–Russia relations") with Russia,[[226]](#cite_note-246) and the two states often vote in unison in the UN Security Council.[[227]](#cite_note-247)[[228]](#cite_note-248) China has provided Russia with economic, technological, and diplomatic support during the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)").[[229]](#cite_note-249)[[230]](#cite_note-250) [China's relationship with the United States](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_relations "China–United States relations") is complex, and includes deep trade ties but significant political differences.[[231]](#cite_note-251) Since the early 2000s, China has followed a policy of [engaging with African nations](/wiki/Sino-African_relations "Sino-African relations") for trade and bilateral co-operation.[[232]](#cite_note-252)[[233]](#cite_note-253)[[234]](#cite_note-254) It maintains extensive and highly diversified trade links with the European Union, and became its largest trading partner for goods.[[235]](#cite_note-qz_EU_trade-255) China is increasing its influence in [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia")[[236]](#cite_note-256) and South Pacific.[[237]](#cite_note-257) The country has strong trade ties with [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN") countries[[238]](#cite_note-258) and major South American economies,[[239]](#cite_note-259) and is the largest trading partner of Brazil, Chile, Peru, Uruguay, Argentina, and several others.[[240]](#cite_note-260)

In 2013, China initiated the [Belt and Road Initiative](/wiki/Belt_and_Road_Initiative "Belt and Road Initiative") (BRI), a large global infrastructure building initiative with funding on the order of $50–100 billion per year.[[241]](#cite_note-261) BRI could be one of the largest development plans in modern history.[[242]](#cite_note-262) It expanded significantly over the next six years and, as of April 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), included 138 countries and 30 international organizations. In addition to intensifying foreign policy relations, the focus is particularly on building efficient transport routes, especially the [maritime Silk Road](/wiki/21st_Century_Maritime_Silk_Road "21st Century Maritime Silk Road") with its connections to East Africa and Europe. However many loans made under the program are unsustainable and China has faced a number of calls for [debt relief](/wiki/Debt_relief "Debt relief") from debtor nations.[[243]](#cite_note-263)[[244]](#cite_note-264)

### Military

Main articles: [Military budget of China](/wiki/Military_budget_of_China "Military budget of China"), [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army"), and [Paramilitary forces of China](/wiki/Paramilitary_forces_of_China "Paramilitary forces of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Quadruple_formation_of_J-20_at_CCAS2023_%2820230724100632%29.jpg/250px-Quadruple_formation_of_J-20_at_CCAS2023_%2820230724100632%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quadruple_formation_of_J-20_at_CCAS2023_(20230724100632).jpg)

[Chengdu J-20](/wiki/Chengdu_J-20 "Chengdu J-20") [5th generation](/wiki/Fifth-generation_fighter "Fifth-generation fighter") stealth fighter

The [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") (PLA) is considered one of the world's most powerful militaries and has [rapidly modernized](/wiki/Modernization_of_the_People%27s_Liberation_Army "Modernization of the People's Liberation Army") in the recent decades.[[245]](#cite_note-265) Per the principle of "[the Party commands the gun](/wiki/The_Party_commands_the_gun "The Party commands the gun")," the CCP maintains absolute control of the PLA.[[246]](#cite_note-266) It consists of four services: the [Ground Force](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Ground_Force "People's Liberation Army Ground Force") (PLAGF), the [Navy](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Navy "People's Liberation Army Navy") (PLAN), the [Air Force](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Air_Force "People's Liberation Army Air Force") (PLAAF) and the [Rocket Force](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Rocket_Force "People's Liberation Army Rocket Force") (PLARF). It also has four independent arms: the [Aerospace Force](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Aerospace_Force "People's Liberation Army Aerospace Force"), the [Cyberspace Force](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Cyberspace_Force "People's Liberation Army Cyberspace Force"), the [Information Support Force](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Information_Support_Force "People's Liberation Army Information Support Force"), and the [Joint Logistics Support Force](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Joint_Logistics_Support_Force "People's Liberation Army Joint Logistics Support Force"), the first three of which were split from the disbanded [Strategic Support Force](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Strategic_Support_Force "People's Liberation Army Strategic Support Force") (PLASSF).[[247]](#cite_note-ChinaMilitary-267) Its 2 million active duty personnel is the [largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_military_and_paramilitary_personnel "List of countries by number of military and paramilitary personnel"). The PLA holds the world's [third-largest stockpile of nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_China "Nuclear weapons of China"),[[248]](#cite_note-268)[[249]](#cite_note-269) and the world's second-largest navy by tonnage.[[250]](#cite_note-270)

China's official [military budget](/wiki/Military_budget_of_China "Military budget of China") for 2024 totalled US$229 billion (1.67 trillion Yuan), the [second-largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_with_highest_military_expenditures "List of countries with highest military expenditures"), though the [Stockholm International Peace Research Institute](/wiki/Stockholm_International_Peace_Research_Institute "Stockholm International Peace Research Institute") estimates that its real expenditure that year was US$314 billion, making up 12% of global military spending and accounting for 1.7% of the country's GDP.***[[251]](#cite_note-SIPRI-2020-271)*** But on a [Purchasing Power Parity](/wiki/Purchasing_Power_Parity "Purchasing Power Parity") basis, China's 2024 military budget was the equivalent of US$567 billion. The PLA is commanded by the [Central Military Commission](/wiki/Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Central Military Commission (China)") (CMC) of the party and the state; though officially two separate organizations, the two CMCs have identical membership except during leadership transition periods and effectively function as one organization. The [chairman of the CMC](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Chairman of the Central Military Commission (China)") is the [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Supreme_command_of_the_armed_forces_in_China "Supreme command of the armed forces in China") of the PLA, who maintains absolute control over the military per the [chairman responsibility system](/wiki/Chairman_responsibility_system "Chairman responsibility system").[[252]](#cite_note-272)

### Sociopolitical issues and human rights

Main articles: [Human rights in China](/wiki/Human_rights_in_China "Human rights in China") and [Censorship in China](/wiki/Censorship_in_China "Censorship in China")

The situation of [human rights in China](/wiki/Human_rights_in_China "Human rights in China") has attracted significant criticism from foreign governments, foreign press agencies, and non-governmental organizations, alleging widespread civil rights violations such as detention without trial, forced confessions, torture, restrictions of fundamental rights, and [excessive use of the death penalty](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_China "Capital punishment in China").[[187]](#cite_note-freedomhouse-206)[[253]](#cite_note-Amnesty-2023-273) Since its inception, [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") has ranked China as "not free" in its *[Freedom in the World](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World "Freedom in the World")* survey,[[187]](#cite_note-freedomhouse-206) while [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") has documented significant human rights abuses.[[253]](#cite_note-Amnesty-2023-273) The [Chinese constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_China "Constitution of China") nevertheless states that the "[fundamental rights](/wiki/Fundamental_rights "Fundamental rights")" of citizens include [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech"), [freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press"), the [right to a fair trial](/wiki/Right_to_a_fair_trial "Right to a fair trial"), [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion"), [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage"), and [property rights](/wiki/Property "Property"). However, in practice, these provisions do not afford significant protection against criminal prosecution by the state.[[254]](#cite_note-274)[[255]](#cite_note-books.google-275)[[256]](#cite_note-276) Although some criticisms of government policies and the ruling CCP are tolerated, [censorship](/wiki/Censorship "Censorship") of political speech and information is amongst the harshest in the world and is routinely used to prevent collective action.[[257]](#cite_note-277) The government suppresses popular protests and demonstrations that it considers a potential threat to "social stability".[[258]](#cite_note-278) China additionally uses a massive surveillance network of cameras, facial recognition software, sensors, and surveillance of personal technology as a means of social control of persons living in the country.[[186]](#cite_note-Isabelle-205)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Xinjiang_Internment_Map%2C_US-Aus_Gov_Assessment.jpg/250px-Xinjiang_Internment_Map%2C_US-Aus_Gov_Assessment.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xinjiang_Internment_Map,_US-Aus_Gov_Assessment.jpg)

In [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang"), China has been accused of detaining more than one million [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs") and other ethnic minorities in camps.

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Hong_Kong_anti-extradition_bill_protest_%2848108594957%29.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong_anti-extradition_bill_protest_%2848108594957%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_anti-extradition_bill_protest_(48108594957).jpg)

[2019–20 Hong Kong protests](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Hong_Kong_protests "2019–20 Hong Kong protests")

China is regularly accused of [large-scale repression](/wiki/Cultural_repression_in_Tibet "Cultural repression in Tibet") and human rights abuses in [Tibet](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Tibet "Human rights in Tibet") and [Xinjiang](/wiki/Persecution_of_Uyghurs_in_China "Persecution of Uyghurs in China"),[[259]](#cite_note-279)[[260]](#cite_note-280)[[261]](#cite_note-281) where significant numbers of ethnic minorities reside, including violent police crackdowns and [religious suppression](/wiki/Religious_suppression "Religious suppression").[[262]](#cite_note-282)[[263]](#cite_note-283) Since 2017, the Chinese government has been engaged in a harsh crackdown in Xinjiang, with around one million [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs") and other ethnic and religion minorities being detained in [internment camps](/wiki/Xinjiang_internment_camps "Xinjiang internment camps") aimed at changing the political thinking of detainees, their identities, and their religious beliefs,[[264]](#cite_note-Graham-Harrison-2019-284) in what some described as a genocide or [crimes against humanity](/wiki/Crimes_against_humanity "Crimes against humanity").[[265]](#cite_note-BBC_News-2021-285)[[266]](#cite_note-286)[[267]](#cite_note-Cumming-Bruce-2022-287) According to reports, political indoctrination, torture, [physical](/wiki/Physical_abuse "Physical abuse") and [psychological](/wiki/Psychological_abuse "Psychological abuse") abuse, [forced sterilization](/wiki/Compulsory_sterilization "Compulsory sterilization"), [sexual abuse](/wiki/Sexual_abuse "Sexual abuse"), and [forced labor](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour") are common in these facilities.[[268]](#cite_note-Khatchadourian-2021-288) The Chinese authorities have also cracked down on dissent in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), especially after the passage of a [national security law](/wiki/2020_Hong_Kong_national_security_law "2020 Hong Kong national security law") in 2020.[[269]](#cite_note-289)

In 2017 and 2020, the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") ranked the severity of Chinese government restrictions on religion as being among the world's highest, despite ranking religious-related social hostilities in China as low in severity.[[270]](#cite_note-290)[[271]](#cite_note-291) China has limited protections regarding [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_China "LGBT rights in China").[[272]](#cite_note-292) The [Global Slavery Index](/wiki/Global_Slavery_Index "Global Slavery Index") estimated that in 2016 more than 3.8 million people (0.25% of the population) were living in "conditions of modern [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_China "Slavery in China")", including victims of human trafficking, forced labor, forced marriage, child labor, and state-imposed forced labor. The state-imposed [re-education through labor](/wiki/Re-education_through_labor "Re-education through labor") (*laojiao*) system was formally abolished in 2013, but it is not clear to what extent its practices have stopped.[[273]](#cite_note-293) The much larger [reform through labor](/wiki/Laogai "Laogai") (*laogai*) system includes labor prison factories, detention centers, and re-education camps; the [Laogai Research Foundation](/wiki/Laogai_Research_Foundation "Laogai Research Foundation") has estimated in June 2008 that there were nearly 1,422 of these facilities, though it cautioned that this number was likely an underestimate.[[274]](#cite_note-294)

### Public views of government

Further information: [Politics of China § Citizen surveys](/wiki/Politics_of_China#Citizen_surveys "Politics of China")

Political concerns in China include the growing gap between rich and poor and government corruption.[[275]](#cite_note-295) Nonetheless, international surveys show the Chinese public have a high level of satisfaction with their government.[[202]](#cite_note-:0-221): 137 These views are generally attributed to the material comforts and security available to large segments of the Chinese populace as well as the government's attentiveness and responsiveness.[[202]](#cite_note-:0-221) : 136 According to the [World Values Survey](/wiki/World_Values_Survey "World Values Survey") (2022), 91% of Chinese respondents have significant confidence in their government.[[202]](#cite_note-:0-221): 13 A Harvard University survey published in July 2020 found that citizen satisfaction with the government had increased since 2003, also rating China's government as more effective and capable than ever in the survey's history.[[276]](#cite_note-296) A 2020 study by [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California") researchers affiliated with the [Hoover Institution](/wiki/Hoover_Institution "Hoover Institution") found that more anonymous surveys show 50 to 70 percent support for the CCP, much lower than what direct surveys show at above 90 percent.[[277]](#cite_note-297)[[278]](#cite_note-:10-298)[[279]](#cite_note-299)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of China](/wiki/Economy_of_China "Economy of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Pudong_Shanghai_November_2017_panorama.jpg/330px-Pudong_Shanghai_November_2017_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pudong_Shanghai_November_2017_panorama.jpg)

Skyline of [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui "Lujiazui") in Shanghai

China has the world's [second-largest economy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") in terms of [nominal GDP](/wiki/Nominal_GDP "Nominal GDP"),[[280]](#cite_note-300) and the world's [largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") in terms of [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") (PPP).[[281]](#cite_note-301) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), China accounts for around 18% of the [global economy](/wiki/World_economy "World economy") by nominal GDP.[[282]](#cite_note-IMF-2023-302) China is one of the world's [fastest-growing](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_real_GDP_growth_rate "List of countries by real GDP growth rate") major economies,[[283]](#cite_note-303) with its economic growth having been almost consistently above 5 percent since the introduction of the [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up") policy in 1978.[[284]](#cite_note-304) According to the World Bank, China's GDP grew from $150 billion in 1978 to $18.74 trillion by 2024.[[285]](#cite_note-305) It ranks [78th by nominal GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita"), making it an upper-middle income country.[[286]](#cite_note-306) Of the world's [500 largest companies](/wiki/Fortune_Global_500 "Fortune Global 500"), 135 are headquartered in China.[[287]](#cite_note-307)

China [was one of the world's foremost economic powers](/wiki/Economic_history_of_China_before_1912 "Economic history of China before 1912") throughout the arc of [East Asian](/wiki/Economy_of_East_Asia#China "Economy of East Asia") and [global history](/wiki/Economic_history_of_China_before_1912 "Economic history of China before 1912"). The country [had one of the largest economies](/wiki/List_of_regions_by_past_GDP_(PPP) "List of regions by past GDP (PPP)") in the world for most of the [past two millennia](/wiki/Pax_Sinica "Pax Sinica"),[[288]](#cite_note-308) during which it has seen cycles of prosperity and decline.[[51]](#cite_note-Dahlman_Aubert_2001-68)[[289]](#cite_note-309) Since economic reforms began in 1978, China has developed into a highly diversified economy and one of the most consequential players in international trade. China officially calls its economic system as the [socialist market economy](/wiki/Socialist_market_economy "Socialist market economy").[[290]](#cite_note-Ding2009-310) Modern-day China is often described as an example of [state capitalism](/wiki/State_capitalism "State capitalism") or [party-state capitalism](/wiki/Party-state_capitalism "Party-state capitalism"), with both market forces and the state playing a major role in the economy.[[291]](#cite_note-Pearson-2021-311)[[292]](#cite_note-312) China has numerous [state-owned enterprises](/wiki/State-owned_enterprises_of_China "State-owned enterprises of China"); the state dominates in strategic "pillar" sectors such as infrastructure, telecommunications, finance, energy production and [heavy industries](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry").[[293]](#cite_note-:4-313): 20 The Chinese government issues [five-year plans](/wiki/Five-year_plans_of_China "Five-year plans of China") to guide the direction of the economy.[[293]](#cite_note-:4-313): 8 Private enterprise has expanded enormously, with around 57 million private businesses recorded in 2025.[[294]](#cite_note-314) According to official statistics, privately owned companies constitute more than 60% of China's GDP.[[295]](#cite_note-315)

### Wealth

See also: [Income inequality in China](/wiki/Income_inequality_in_China "Income inequality in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/GDP_per_capita_development_in_China.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_development_in_China.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_in_China.svg)

GDP per capita in China, from 1850 to 2022

China [accounted for 18.6%](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_total_wealth "List of countries by total wealth") of the world's total wealth in 2022, second highest in the world after the U.S.[[296]](#cite_note-databook2023-316) China brought more people out of extreme [poverty](/wiki/Poverty_in_China "Poverty in China") than any other country in history[[297]](#cite_note-317)[[298]](#cite_note-318)—between 1978 and 2018, China reduced extreme poverty by 800 million,[[202]](#cite_note-:0-221): 23 with the average standard of living multiplying by a factor of twenty-six.[[299]](#cite_note-Bergsten_2022-319) From 1990 to 2018, the proportion of the Chinese population living with an income of less than $1.90 per day (2011 [PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity")) decreased from 66.3% to 0.3%, the share living with an income of less than $3.20 per day from 90.0% to 2.9%, and the share living with an income of less than $5.50 per day decreased from 98.3% to 17.0%.[[300]](#cite_note-320) Per capita incomes have risen significantly – when the PRC was founded in 1949, per capita income in China was one-fifth of the world average; per capita incomes now equal the world average itself.[[299]](#cite_note-Bergsten_2022-319)

China's development is highly uneven; its major cities and coastal areas are far more prosperous than its rural and interior regions.[[301]](#cite_note-321) It has a high level of economic inequality,[[302]](#cite_note-322) which has increased quickly since the economic reforms.[[303]](#cite_note-323) [Income inequality](/wiki/Income_inequality "Income inequality") decreased in the 2010s,[[304]](#cite_note-324) and China's [Gini coefficient](/wiki/Gini_coefficient "Gini coefficient") was 0.37 in 2023.[[10]](#cite_note-GINI-16) In March 2025, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* estimated China ranked second in the world, after the U.S., in [total number of billionaires](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_billionaires "List of countries by number of billionaires") and [total number of millionaires](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_millionaires "List of countries by number of millionaires"), with 516 Chinese billionaires,[[305]](#cite_note-325) while *Hurun Global Rich List* estimated it ranked first, with 1,021 billionaires.[[306]](#cite_note-326) China also has 6.3 million millionaires as of 2025, second highest after the U.S.[[307]](#cite_note-327) In 2019, China overtook the U.S. as the home to the highest number of people who have a net personal wealth of at least $110,000, according to the global wealth report by [Credit Suisse](/wiki/Credit_Suisse "Credit Suisse").[[308]](#cite_note-328)[[309]](#cite_note-329) China had 85 female billionaires as of January 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), two-thirds of the global total.[[310]](#cite_note-330) China has had the world's largest middle-class population since 2015;[[311]](#cite_note-331) the middle-class grew to 500 million by 2024.[[312]](#cite_note-332)

### Industry and services

China is the world's leading manufacturing power, accounting for 30% of global manufacturing.[[313]](#cite_note-333)[[314]](#cite_note-334) It has been the world's [largest manufacturing nation](/wiki/Manufacturing#List_of_countries_by_manufacturing_output "Manufacturing") since 2010, after overtaking the U.S., which had been the largest for the previous hundred years.[[315]](#cite_note-335)[[316]](#cite_note-336) China is the world's leading producer of [steel](/wiki/Steel_industry_in_China "Steel industry in China") and [rare earths](/wiki/Rare-earth_industry_in_China "Rare-earth industry in China"),[[317]](#cite_note-337) the world's leading [electronics industry manufacturer](/wiki/Electronics_industry_in_China "Electronics industry in China"),[[318]](#cite_note-338) and the world's dominant shipbuilding manufacturer.[[319]](#cite_note-339) China has the world's largest market for automobiles,[[320]](#cite_note-340)[[321]](#cite_note-341) having surpassed the United States in both auto sales and [production](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_motor_vehicle_production "List of countries by motor vehicle production"). The country is the world's largest exporter of cars by number as of 2024.[[322]](#cite_note-342) The [Chinese automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_China "Automotive industry in China") is regarded as one of the most competitive and innovative in the world.[[168]](#cite_note-:37-185) China is the world's leader in electric vehicle consumption, production and innovation,[[323]](#cite_note-343)[[324]](#cite_note-:5-344) as well as the leading producer of batteries for electric vehicles.[[325]](#cite_note-345)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/2023_BYD_Song_Plus_DM-i_%28front%29.jpg/250px-2023_BYD_Song_Plus_DM-i_%28front%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2023_BYD_Song_Plus_DM-i_(front).jpg)

The [BYD Song Plus](/wiki/BYD_Song_Plus "BYD Song Plus"), a [compact crossover SUV](/wiki/Compact_crossover_SUV "Compact crossover SUV") manufactured by [BYD Auto](/wiki/BYD_Auto "BYD Auto"). China is the [largest producer of motor vehicles](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_motor_vehicle_production "List of countries by motor vehicle production") in the world.[[326]](#cite_note-:02-346)

China is the second-largest retail market after the United States.[[327]](#cite_note-347) China leads the world in e-commerce, accounting for over 37% of the global market share in 2021.[[328]](#cite_note-348) China has three out of the ten [largest stock exchanges](/wiki/List_of_major_stock_exchanges "List of major stock exchanges") in the world[[329]](#cite_note-349)—[Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai_Stock_Exchange "Shanghai Stock Exchange"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stock_Exchange "Hong Kong Stock Exchange") and [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen_Stock_Exchange "Shenzhen Stock Exchange")—that together have a market capitalization of over $15.9 trillion, as of October 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit).[[330]](#cite_note-350) China has three out of the world's ten most competitive financial centers according to the 2024 [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index")—[Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), and [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen").[[331]](#cite_note-GFCI362-351) As of at least 2024, China has the world's second-largest equity markets and futures markets, as well as the third-largest bond market.[[332]](#cite_note-:Curtis&Klaus2-352): 153 China has the world's largest [banking sector](/wiki/Banking_in_China "Banking in China"). Its finance sector is dominated by state-owned institutions.[[333]](#cite_note-353)

### China in the global economy

China has been a member of the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") since 2001 and is the world's [largest trading power](/wiki/List_of_largest_trading_countries "List of largest trading countries").[[334]](#cite_note-354) By 2016, China was the largest trading partner of 124 countries.[[335]](#cite_note-355) China became the world's largest trading nation in 2013 by the sum of imports and exports, as well as the world's largest commodity importer, accounting for roughly 45% of maritime's [dry-bulk market](/wiki/Shipping_markets "Shipping markets").[[336]](#cite_note-356)[[337]](#cite_note-357) [China's foreign exchange reserves](/wiki/Foreign-exchange_reserves_of_China "Foreign-exchange reserves of China") reached US$3.246 trillion as of March 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), making its reserves by far the world's largest.[[338]](#cite_note-358) In 2022, China was amongst the world's largest recipient of inward [foreign direct investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment") (FDI), attracting $180 billion, though most of these were speculated to be from Hong Kong.[[339]](#cite_note-359) China also invests abroad, with a total outward FDI of $147.9 billion in 2023.[[340]](#cite_note-360)

The Chinese government has promoted the [internationalization of the renminbi](/wiki/Internationalization_of_the_renminbi "Internationalization of the renminbi") in order to wean itself off its dependence on the U.S. dollar.[[341]](#cite_note-361) The renminbi is the world's fourth-most traded currency as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit).[[342]](#cite_note-362) However, partly due to capital controls that make the renminbi fall short of being a fully convertible currency, it remains far behind the Euro and the U.S. Dollar in international trade volumes.[[343]](#cite_note-363) The Chinese government has also been cited for failing to crack down on the manufacturing and export of [counterfeit](/wiki/Counterfeit "Counterfeit") goods.[[344]](#cite_note-364) In 2023, [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University")'s [Economic Complexity Index](/wiki/Economic_Complexity_Index "Economic Complexity Index") ranked complexity of China's exports 16th in the world, up from 24th in 2010.[[345]](#cite_note-365)

### Tourism

Main articles: [Tourism in China](/wiki/Tourism_in_China "Tourism in China") and [List of World Heritage Sites in China](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_China "List of World Heritage Sites in China")

[![The Forbidden City is one of the most popular tourist attractions in the world](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Hall_of_Supreme_Harmony_%2820241127120000%29.jpg/250px-Hall_of_Supreme_Harmony_%2820241127120000%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hall_of_Supreme_Harmony_(20241127120000).jpg)

The [Forbidden City](/wiki/Forbidden_City "Forbidden City") is one of the most popular tourist attractions in the world.

China received 65.7 million international visitors in 2019,[[346]](#cite_note-WTO_Tourism_Highlights_2019_Edition-366) and in 2018 was the [fourth-most-visited country](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings") in the world.[[346]](#cite_note-WTO_Tourism_Highlights_2019_Edition-366) It also experiences an enormous volume of [domestic tourism](/wiki/Domestic_tourism "Domestic tourism"); Chinese tourists made an estimated 6 billion travels within the country in 2019.[[347]](#cite_note-367) China hosts the world's [second-largest number](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_by_country#Countries_with_major_concentrations_of_World_Heritage_Sites "World Heritage Sites by country") of [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") ([60](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_China "List of World Heritage Sites in China")) after Italy, and is one of the [most popular tourist destinations](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings") ([first in the Asia-Pacific](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings#Asia-Pacific "World Tourism rankings")).

### Science and technology

Main articles: [Science and technology in China](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_China "Science and technology in China"), [List of Chinese discoveries](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_discoveries "List of Chinese discoveries"), and [List of Chinese inventions](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_inventions "List of Chinese inventions")

#### Historical

Main article: [History of science and technology in China](/wiki/History_of_science_and_technology_in_China "History of science and technology in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Chinese_Gunpowder_Formula.JPG/250px-Chinese_Gunpowder_Formula.JPG)](/wiki/File:Chinese_Gunpowder_Formula.JPG)

Earliest known written [formula for gunpowder](/wiki/History_of_gunpowder "History of gunpowder"), from the *[Wujing Zongyao](/wiki/Wujing_Zongyao "Wujing Zongyao")* of 1044 CE

China was a world leader in science and technology until the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty").[[348]](#cite_note-368) Ancient and medieval [Chinese discoveries](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_discoveries "List of Chinese discoveries") and [inventions](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_inventions "List of Chinese inventions"), such as [papermaking](/wiki/Papermaking "Papermaking"), [printing](/wiki/History_of_typography_in_East_Asia "History of typography in East Asia"), the [compass](/wiki/Compass "Compass"), and [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") (the [Four Great Inventions](/wiki/Four_Great_Inventions "Four Great Inventions")), became widespread across East Asia, the Middle East and later Europe. Chinese mathematicians were the first to use [negative numbers](/wiki/Negative_numbers#History "Negative numbers").[[349]](#cite_note-369)[[350]](#cite_note-370) By the 17th century, the Western World surpassed China in scientific and technological advancement.[[351]](#cite_note-371) The causes of this early modern [Great Divergence](/wiki/Great_Divergence "Great Divergence") continue to be debated by scholars.[[352]](#cite_note-372)

After [repeated military defeats](/wiki/Century_of_humiliation "Century of humiliation") by the [European colonial powers](/wiki/Eight-Nation_Alliance "Eight-Nation Alliance") and [Imperial Japan](/wiki/First_Sino-Japanese_War "First Sino-Japanese War") in the 19th century, Chinese reformers began promoting modern science and technology as part of the [Self-Strengthening Movement](/wiki/Self-Strengthening_Movement "Self-Strengthening Movement"). After the Communists came to power in 1949, efforts were made to organize science and technology based on the model of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), in which scientific research was part of central planning.[[353]](#cite_note-373) After Mao's death in 1976, science and technology were promoted as one of the [Four Modernizations](/wiki/Four_Modernizations "Four Modernizations"),[[354]](#cite_note-374) and the Soviet-inspired academic system was gradually reformed.[[355]](#cite_note-375)

#### Modern era

Since the end of the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), China has made significant investments in scientific research[[356]](#cite_note-CWRD-376) and is quickly catching up with the U.S. [in R&D spending](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_research_and_development_spending "List of sovereign states by research and development spending").[[357]](#cite_note-377)[[358]](#cite_note-378) China officially spent around 2.7% of its GDP on R&D in 2024, totaling to around $496 billion.[[359]](#cite_note-379) China was ranked 10th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025,[[360]](#cite_note-380)[[361]](#cite_note-381) a considerable improvement from its rank of 35th in 2013.[[362]](#cite_note-382)[[363]](#cite_note-383) [Chinese supercomputers](/wiki/Supercomputing_in_China "Supercomputing in China") are ranked among the [fastest in the world](/wiki/TOP500 "TOP500").[[364]](#cite_note-384)[[u]](#cite_note-385) China is the world's largest [industrial robotics](/wiki/Industrial_robot "Industrial robot") producer and user in the world, accounting for 51% of the world's 2024 total demand,[[365]](#cite_note-386) and 43% of global production.[[324]](#cite_note-:5-344) Its efforts to develop the most advanced semiconductors and jet engines have seen delays and setbacks.[[366]](#cite_note-387)[[367]](#cite_note-388) China is also considered a world leader in [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence_industry_in_China "Artificial intelligence industry in China").[[368]](#cite_note-389)[[369]](#cite_note-390)

China is developing [its education system](/wiki/Education_in_China "Education in China") with an emphasis on [science, technology, engineering, and mathematics](/wiki/Science,_technology,_engineering,_and_mathematics "Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics") (STEM).[[370]](#cite_note-391) [Its academic publication apparatus](/wiki/Academic_publishing_in_China "Academic publishing in China") became the world's [largest publisher of scientific papers](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_scientific_and_technical_journal_articles "List of countries by number of scientific and technical journal articles") in 2016.[[371]](#cite_note-392)[[372]](#cite_note-393)[[373]](#cite_note-394) According to the [World Intellectual Property Indicators](/wiki/World_Intellectual_Property_Indicators "World Intellectual Property Indicators"), China received more applications than the U.S. did in 2018 and 2019 and ranked first globally in patents, utility models, trademarks, industrial designs, and creative goods exports in 2021.[[374]](#cite_note-Dutta-2021-395)[[375]](#cite_note-396)[[376]](#cite_note-397) In 2022, China overtook the US in the [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index"), which measures the share of published articles in leading scientific journals.[[377]](#cite_note-398)[[378]](#cite_note-399)

##### Space program

Main article: [Chinese space program](/wiki/Chinese_space_program "Chinese space program")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Launch_of_Shenzhou_13.jpg/250px-Launch_of_Shenzhou_13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Launch_of_Shenzhou_13.jpg)

Launch of [Shenzhou 13](/wiki/Shenzhou_13 "Shenzhou 13") by a [Long March 2F](/wiki/Long_March_2F "Long March 2F") rocket. China is one of the only three countries with independent [human spaceflight](/wiki/Human_spaceflight "Human spaceflight") capability.

The Chinese space program started in 1958 with some technology transfers from the Soviet Union. However, it did not launch the nation's first satellite until 1970 with the [Dong Fang Hong I](/wiki/Dong_Fang_Hong_I "Dong Fang Hong I"), which made China the fifth country to do so independently.[[379]](#cite_note-400)

In 2003, China became the third country in the world to independently send humans into space with [Yang Liwei](/wiki/Yang_Liwei "Yang Liwei")'s spaceflight aboard [Shenzhou 5](/wiki/Shenzhou_5 "Shenzhou 5"). As of 2023, [eighteen Chinese nationals](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_astronauts "List of Chinese astronauts") have journeyed into space, including two women. In 2011, China launched its first space station testbed, [Tiangong-1](/wiki/Tiangong-1 "Tiangong-1").[[380]](#cite_note-401) In 2013, a Chinese robotic rover *[Yutu](/wiki/Yutu_(rover) "Yutu (rover)")* successfully touched down on the lunar surface as part of the [Chang'e 3](/wiki/Chang%27e_3 "Chang'e 3") mission.[[381]](#cite_note-402) In 2019, China became the first country to land a probe—[Chang'e 4](/wiki/Chang%27e_4 "Chang'e 4")—on the [far side of the Moon](/wiki/Far_side_of_the_Moon "Far side of the Moon").[[382]](#cite_note-403) In 2020, [Chang'e 5](/wiki/Chang%27e_5 "Chang'e 5") successfully returned Moon samples to the Earth, making China the third country to do so independently.[[383]](#cite_note-404) In 2021, China became the third country to land a spacecraft on Mars and the second one to deploy a [rover (*Zhurong*)](/wiki/Zhurong_(rover) "Zhurong (rover)") on Mars.[[384]](#cite_note-405) China completed its own modular [space station](/wiki/Space_station "Space station"), the [Tiangong](/wiki/Tiangong_space_station "Tiangong space station"), in [low Earth orbit](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit") on 3 November 2022.[[385]](#cite_note-406)[[386]](#cite_note-407)[[387]](#cite_note-408) On 29 November 2022, China performed its first in-orbit crew handover aboard the *Tiangong*.[[388]](#cite_note-409)[[389]](#cite_note-410)

In May 2023, China announced a plan to [land humans on the Moon](/wiki/Moon_landing "Moon landing") by 2030.[[390]](#cite_note-411) To that end, China has been developing a lunar-capable super-heavy launcher, the [Long March 10](/wiki/Long_March_10 "Long March 10"), a new [crewed spacecraft](/wiki/Next-generation_crewed_spacecraft "Next-generation crewed spacecraft"), and a [crewed lunar lander](/wiki/Chinese_crewed_lunar_lander "Chinese crewed lunar lander").[[391]](#cite_note-AJ-06Mar2022-412)[[392]](#cite_note-AJ17072023-413) China sent [Chang'e 6](/wiki/Chang%27e_6 "Chang'e 6") on 3 May 2024, which conducted the first lunar sample return from [Apollo Basin](/wiki/Apollo_(crater) "Apollo (crater)") on the [far side of the Moon](/wiki/Far_side_of_the_Moon "Far side of the Moon").[[393]](#cite_note-AJ_FI-20230425-414) This is China's second lunar sample return mission, the first was achieved by [Chang'e 5](/wiki/Chang%27e_5 "Chang'e 5") from the lunar near side 4 years ago.[[394]](#cite_note-415) It also carried a Chinese rover called *Jinchan* to conduct [infrared spectroscopy](/wiki/Absorption_spectroscopy "Absorption spectroscopy") of lunar surface and imaged Chang'e 6 lander on lunar surface.[[395]](#cite_note-416)

## Infrastructure

After a decades-long infrastructural boom,[[396]](#cite_note-417) China has produced numerous world-leading infrastructural projects: it has the [largest high-speed rail network](/wiki/List_of_high-speed_railway_lines_in_China "List of high-speed railway lines in China"),[[397]](#cite_note-418) the [most supertall skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers "List of supertall skyscrapers"),[[398]](#cite_note-419) the largest power plant (the [Three Gorges Dam](/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam "Three Gorges Dam")),[[399]](#cite_note-420) the most extensive [ultra-high-voltage transmission network](/wiki/Ultra-high-voltage_electricity_transmission_in_China "Ultra-high-voltage electricity transmission in China") and innovation infrastructure,[[400]](#cite_note-Vara_2022-421)[[401]](#cite_note-You_2024-422) and [a global satellite navigation system](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou") with the largest number of satellites.[[402]](#cite_note-423)

### Telecommunications

Main article: [Telecommunications in China](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_China "Telecommunications in China")

[China Mobile](/wiki/China_Mobile "China Mobile") built a 5G station to cover summit of [Mount Everest](/wiki/Mount_Everest "Mount Everest") in 2020

China is the largest telecom market in the world and currently has the [largest number of active cellphones of any country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_mobile_numbers_in_use "List of countries by number of mobile numbers in use"), with over 1.7 billion subscribers, as of February 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit). It has the largest number of [internet](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_Internet_users "List of countries by number of Internet users") and [broadband users](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_number_of_broadband_Internet_subscriptions "List of sovereign states by number of broadband Internet subscriptions"), with over 1.125 billion Internet users as of December 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit)—equivalent to around 80.1% of its population.[[403]](#cite_note-424) By 2018, China had more than 1 billion 4G users, accounting for 40% of world's total.[[404]](#cite_note-425) As of December 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), China had over 810 million [5G](/wiki/5G "5G") users and 3.38 million base stations installed.[[405]](#cite_note-426) [China Mobile](/wiki/China_Mobile "China Mobile"), [China Unicom](/wiki/China_Unicom "China Unicom") and [China Telecom](/wiki/China_Telecom "China Telecom"), are the three large providers of mobile and internet in China. Combined, the three operators had over 3.4 million 4G base-stations in China.[[406]](#cite_note-TechNode-2018-427) China has developed its own [satellite navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") system, dubbed [BeiDou](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou"), which began offering commercial navigation services across Asia in 2012[[407]](#cite_note-CustomersDec2012-428) as well as global services by the end of 2018.[[408]](#cite_note-429) BeiDou followed [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") and [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS") as the third completed global navigation satellite.[[409]](#cite_note-430)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in China](/wiki/Transport_in_China "Transport in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Huajiang_Canyon_Bridge2.JPG/250px-Huajiang_Canyon_Bridge2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Huajiang_Canyon_Bridge2.JPG)

The [Huajiang Canyon Bridge](/wiki/Huajiang_Canyon_Bridge "Huajiang Canyon Bridge") is the [highest bridge](/wiki/List_of_highest_bridges "List of highest bridges") in the world.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/CR400BF-Z-0312%40BJI_%2820231009152047%29.jpg/250px-CR400BF-Z-0312%40BJI_%2820231009152047%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:CR400BF-Z-0312@BJI_(20231009152047).jpg)

A [Fuxing](/wiki/Fuxing_(train) "Fuxing (train)") high-speed train running near the [Beijing CBD](/wiki/Beijing_central_business_district "Beijing central business district")

Since the late 1990s, China's national road network has been significantly expanded through the creation of a network of [national highways](/wiki/National_highways_of_China "National highways of China") and [expressways](/wiki/Expressways_of_China "Expressways of China"). In 2022, China's highways had reached a total length of 177,000 km (110,000 mi), making it the [longest highway system](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_road_network_size "List of countries by road network size") in the world.[[410]](#cite_note-431) In urban areas, bicycles remain a common mode of transport, despite the increasing prevalence of automobiles – as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), there are approximately 200 million bicycles in China.[[411]](#cite_note-432)

[China's railways](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_China "Rail transport in China"), which are operated by the state-owned [China Railway](/wiki/China_Railway "China Railway"), are among [the busiest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_rail_usage "List of countries by rail usage") in the world.[[412]](#cite_note-433) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), the country had 165,000 km (102,526 mi) of railways, the [second-longest network](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_rail_transport_network_size "List of countries by rail transport network size") in the world.[[413]](#cite_note-:6-434) The railways strain to meet enormous demand particularly during the [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year") holiday, when the world's largest annual human migration called [Chunyun](/wiki/Chunyun "Chunyun") takes place.[[414]](#cite_note-overcrowding-435) China's [high-speed rail (HSR) system](/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_China "High-speed rail in China") started construction in the early 2000s. By the end of 2025, high speed rail in China had reached 50,400 kilometers (31,317 miles) of dedicated lines alone, making it the [longest HSR network](/wiki/List_of_high-speed_railway_lines "List of high-speed railway lines") in the world.[[413]](#cite_note-:6-434) Services on the [Beijing–Shanghai](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Shanghai_high-speed_railway "Beijing–Shanghai high-speed railway"), [Beijing–Tianjin](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Tianjin_intercity_railway "Beijing–Tianjin intercity railway"), and [Chengdu–Chongqing](/wiki/Chengdu%E2%80%93Chongqing_intercity_railway "Chengdu–Chongqing intercity railway") lines reach up to 350 km/h (217 mph), making them the fastest conventional high speed railway services in the world. With an annual ridership of over 3.3 billion passengers in 2024, it is the world's busiest.[[415]](#cite_note-436) The network includes the [Beijing–Guangzhou high-speed railway](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Guangzhou_high-speed_railway "Beijing–Guangzhou high-speed railway"), the single longest HSR line in the world, and the [Beijing–Shanghai high-speed railway](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Shanghai_high-speed_railway "Beijing–Shanghai high-speed railway"), which has [three of longest railroad bridges in the world](/wiki/List_of_longest_bridges "List of longest bridges").[[416]](#cite_note-437) The [Shanghai maglev train](/wiki/Shanghai_maglev_train "Shanghai maglev train"), which reaches 431 km/h (268 mph), is the fastest commercial train service in the world.[[417]](#cite_note-438) Since 2000, the growth of rapid transit systems in Chinese cities has accelerated.[[418]](#cite_note-439) As of December 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), 55 Chinese cities have [urban mass transit systems](/wiki/Urban_rail_transit_in_China "Urban rail transit in China") in operation.[[419]](#cite_note-440) As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), China boasts the five longest [metro systems](/wiki/List_of_metro_systems "List of metro systems") in the world with the networks in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai_Metro "Shanghai Metro"), [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing_Subway "Beijing Subway"), [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou_Metro "Guangzhou Metro"), [Chengdu](/wiki/Chengdu_Metro "Chengdu Metro") and [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen_Metro "Shenzhen Metro") being the largest.

The [civil aviation industry in China](/wiki/Civil_aviation_in_China "Civil aviation in China") is mostly state-dominated, with the Chinese government retaining a majority stake in the majority of Chinese airlines. The top three airlines in China are [Air China](/wiki/Air_China "Air China"), [China Southern Airlines](/wiki/China_Southern_Airlines "China Southern Airlines"), and [China Eastern Airlines](/wiki/China_Eastern_Airlines "China Eastern Airlines"), which collectively made up 71% of the market in 2018, are all state-owned.[[420]](#cite_note-441) Air travel has expanded rapidly in the last decades, with the number of passengers increasing from 16.6 million in 1990 to 551.2 million in 2017.[[421]](#cite_note-442) China had [approximately 259 airports in 2024](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_China "List of airports in China").[[422]](#cite_note-443) China has [over 2,000 river and seaports](/wiki/List_of_ports_in_China "List of ports in China"), about 130 of which are open to foreign shipping.[[423]](#cite_note-444) Of the [fifty busiest container ports](/wiki/List_of_busiest_container_ports "List of busiest container ports"), 18 are located in China, of which the busiest is the [Port of Shanghai](/wiki/Port_of_Shanghai "Port of Shanghai"), also the busiest port in the world.[[424]](#cite_note-445) The country's inland waterways are the world's [sixth-longest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_waterways_length "List of countries by waterways length"), and total 27,700 km (17,212 mi).[[425]](#cite_note-446)

### Water supply and sanitation

Main article: [Water supply and sanitation in China](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_China "Water supply and sanitation in China")

Water supply and sanitation infrastructure in China is facing challenges such as rapid urbanization, as well as [water scarcity, contamination, and pollution](/wiki/Water_resources_of_China "Water resources of China").[[426]](#cite_note-Water_Scarcity_in_China-447) According to the [Joint Monitoring Program for Water Supply and Sanitation](/wiki/Joint_Monitoring_Programme_for_Water_Supply_and_Sanitation "Joint Monitoring Programme for Water Supply and Sanitation"), 93% of rural households had access to [basic sanitation](/wiki/Basic_sanitation "Basic sanitation") in 2022 (up from 77% in 2015).[[427]](#cite_note-448) The ongoing [South–North Water Transfer Project](/wiki/South%E2%80%93North_Water_Transfer_Project "South–North Water Transfer Project") intends to abate water shortage in the north.[[428]](#cite_note-449)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of China](/wiki/Demographics_of_China "Demographics of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/China_Population_Density_Map_2020.png/330px-China_Population_Density_Map_2020.png)](/wiki/File:China_Population_Density_Map_2020.png)

Map of China's population density across third-level administrative divisions. Based on the results of the 2020 census.

The [2020 Chinese census](/wiki/2020_Chinese_census "2020 Chinese census") recorded the population as approximately 1,411,778,724. About 17.95% were 14 years old or younger, 63.35% were between 15 and 59 years old, and 18.7% were over 60 years old.[[429]](#cite_note-2020_census2-450) Between 2010 and 2020, the average population growth rate was 0.53%.[[429]](#cite_note-2020_census2-450) Since 2022, deaths have outpaced births in the country.[[430]](#cite_note-:63-451) In 2023, the [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") was reported to be 1.09, ranking [among the lowest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_total_fertility_rate "List of countries by total fertility rate").[[431]](#cite_note-452) In 2023, [National Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/National_Bureau_of_Statistics_of_China "National Bureau of Statistics of China") estimated that the population fell 850,000 from 2021 to 2022, the first decline since 1961.[[432]](#cite_note-453) In 2025, China's population officially stood at 1.404 billion with births at 7.92 million, the lowest recorded birthrate since at least 1949.[[430]](#cite_note-:63-451)

Given concerns about population growth, China implemented a two-child limit during the 1970s, and, in 1979, began to advocate for an even stricter limit of one child per family. Beginning in the mid-1980s, however, given the unpopularity of the strict limits, China began to allow some major exemptions, particularly in rural areas, resulting in what was actually a "1.5"-child policy from the mid-1980s to 2015; ethnic minorities were also exempt from one-child limits.[[433]](#cite_note-:13-454) The next major loosening of the policy was enacted in December 2013, allowing families to have two children if one parent is an only child.[[434]](#cite_note-455) In 2016, the one-child policy was replaced in favor of a [two-child policy](/wiki/Two-child_policy "Two-child policy").[[435]](#cite_note-Birtles-2021-456) A [three-child policy](/wiki/Three-child_policy "Three-child policy") was announced in May 2021, due to [population aging](/wiki/Aging_of_China "Aging of China"),[[435]](#cite_note-Birtles-2021-456) and in July 2021, all family size limits as well as penalties for exceeding them were removed.[[436]](#cite_note-457)

According to one group of scholars, one-child limits had little effect on population growth or total population size.[[437]](#cite_note-Whyte-458)[[438]](#cite_note-Wang_Judge-459) However, these scholars have been challenged.[[439]](#cite_note-460) The policy, along with traditional preference for boys, may have contributed to an imbalance in the [sex ratio](/wiki/Human_sex_ratio "Human sex ratio") at birth.[[440]](#cite_note-461)[[441]](#cite_note-Ref_2007a-462) The 2020 census found that males accounted for 51.2% of the total population.[[442]](#cite_note-NBS_China-2021-463) However, China's sex ratio is more balanced than it was in 1953, when males accounted for 51.8% of the population.[[443]](#cite_note-464) China maintains a restrictive [immigration policy](/wiki/Immigration_to_China "Immigration to China"), with permanent residence granted to only around 12,000 foreigners as of 2023.[[444]](#cite_note-465)
