### Ethnic groups

Main articles: [List of ethnic groups in China](/wiki/List_of_ethnic_groups_in_China "List of ethnic groups in China"), [Ethnic minorities in China](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities_in_China "Ethnic minorities in China"), and [Ethnic groups in Chinese history](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Chinese_history "Ethnic groups in Chinese history")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/China_ethnolinguistic_1967.jpg/250px-China_ethnolinguistic_1967.jpg)](/wiki/File:China_ethnolinguistic_1967.jpg)

Ethnolinguistic map of China in 1967

China legally recognizes 56 distinct ethnic groups, who comprise the *[Zhonghua minzu](/wiki/Zhonghua_minzu "Zhonghua minzu")*. The largest of these nationalities are the [Han Chinese](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese"), who constitute more than 91% of the total population.[[429]](#cite_note-2020_census2-450) The Han Chinese – the world's largest single ethnic group[[461]](#cite_note-484) – outnumber other ethnic groups in every place excluding [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet_Autonomous_Region "Tibet Autonomous Region"), [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang"),[[462]](#cite_note-485) [Linxia](/wiki/Linxia_City "Linxia City"),[[463]](#cite_note-486) and [autonomous prefectures](/wiki/Autonomous_prefecture "Autonomous prefecture") like [Xishuangbanna](/wiki/Xishuangbanna_Dai_Autonomous_Prefecture "Xishuangbanna Dai Autonomous Prefecture").[[464]](#cite_note-487) Ethnic minorities account for less than 10% of the population of China, according to the 2020 census.[[429]](#cite_note-2020_census2-450) Compared with the 2010 population census, the Han population increased by 60,378,693 persons, or 4.93%, while the population of the 55 national minorities combined increased by 11,675,179 persons, or 10.26%.[[429]](#cite_note-2020_census2-450) The 2020 census recorded a total of 845,697 foreign nationals living in mainland China.[[465]](#cite_note-488)

### Languages

Main articles: [Languages of China](/wiki/Languages_of_China "Languages of China") and [List of endangered languages in China](/wiki/List_of_endangered_languages_in_China "List of endangered languages in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Lihaozhai_High_School_-_P1360829.JPG/250px-Lihaozhai_High_School_-_P1360829.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lihaozhai_High_School_-_P1360829.JPG)

A sign at a high school in [Jianshui](/wiki/Jianshui "Jianshui"), Yunnan, written in [Hani](/wiki/Hani_language "Hani language") using the Latin alphabet, [Nisu](/wiki/Nisu_language "Nisu language") using the [Yi script](/wiki/Yi_script "Yi script"), and Chinese

There are as many as 284 [living languages](/wiki/Living_language "Living language") in China.[[466]](#cite_note-489) The languages most commonly spoken belong to the [Sinitic branch](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages") of the [Sino-Tibetan language family](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_language_family "Sino-Tibetan language family"), which contains [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese"), spoken by 80% of the population,[[467]](#cite_note-490)[[468]](#cite_note-491) and [other varieties](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") of [Chinese language](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): [Jin](/wiki/Jin_Chinese "Jin Chinese"), [Wu](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese"), [Min](/wiki/Min_Chinese "Min Chinese"), [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese"), [Yue](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese"), [Xiang](/wiki/Xiang_Chinese "Xiang Chinese"), [Gan](/wiki/Gan_Chinese "Gan Chinese"), [Hui](/wiki/Huizhou_Chinese "Huizhou Chinese"), [Ping](/wiki/Pinghua "Pinghua") and unclassified Tuhua ([Shaozhou Tuhua](/wiki/Shaozhou_Tuhua "Shaozhou Tuhua") and [Xiangnan Tuhua](/wiki/Xiangnan_Tuhua "Xiangnan Tuhua")).[[469]](#cite_note-492) Languages of the [Tibeto-Burman branch](/wiki/Tibeto-Burman_languages "Tibeto-Burman languages"), including [Tibetan](/wiki/Lhasa_Tibetan "Lhasa Tibetan"), [Qiang](/wiki/Qiang_language "Qiang language"), [Naxi](/wiki/Naxi_language "Naxi language") and [Yi](/wiki/Nuosu_language "Nuosu language"), are spoken across the [Tibetan](/wiki/Tibetan_Plateau "Tibetan Plateau") and [Yunnan–Guizhou Plateau](/wiki/Yunnan%E2%80%93Guizhou_Plateau "Yunnan–Guizhou Plateau"). Other ethnic minority languages in [southwestern China](/wiki/Southwestern_China "Southwestern China") include [Zhuang](/wiki/Zhuang_languages "Zhuang languages"), [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"), [Dong](/wiki/Kam_language "Kam language") and [Sui](/wiki/Sui_language "Sui language") of the [Tai-Kadai family](/wiki/Kra%E2%80%93Dai_languages "Kra–Dai languages"), [Miao](/wiki/Hmongic_languages "Hmongic languages") and [Yao](/wiki/Mienic_languages "Mienic languages") of the [Hmong–Mien family](/wiki/Hmong%E2%80%93Mien_languages "Hmong–Mien languages"), and [Wa](/wiki/Wa_language "Wa language") of the [Austroasiatic family](/wiki/Austroasiatic_languages "Austroasiatic languages"). Across [northeastern](/wiki/Northeast_China "Northeast China") and [northwestern China](/wiki/Northwestern_China "Northwestern China"), local ethnic groups speak [Altaic languages](/wiki/Altaic_languages "Altaic languages") including [Manchu](/wiki/Manchu_language "Manchu language"), [Mongolian](/wiki/Mongolian_language "Mongolian language") and several [Turkic languages](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages"): [Uyghur](/wiki/Uyghur_language "Uyghur language"), [Kazakh](/wiki/Kazakh_language "Kazakh language"), [Kyrgyz](/wiki/Kyrgyz_language "Kyrgyz language"), [Salar](/wiki/Salar_language "Salar language") and [Western Yugur](/wiki/Western_Yugur_language "Western Yugur language").[[470]](#cite_note-493) [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language") is spoken natively along the border with [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"). [Sarikoli](/wiki/Sarikoli_language "Sarikoli language"), the language of [Tajiks in China](/wiki/Tajiks_in_China "Tajiks in China"), is an [Indo-European language](/wiki/Indo-European_language "Indo-European language").[[471]](#cite_note-494) [Taiwanese indigenous peoples](/wiki/Taiwanese_indigenous_peoples "Taiwanese indigenous peoples"), including a small population on the mainland, speak [Austronesian languages](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages").[[472]](#cite_note-language-495)

[Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"), a variety based on the [Beijing dialect](/wiki/Beijing_dialect "Beijing dialect") of Mandarin, is the national language of China, having de facto official status.[[2]](#cite_note-Adamson_&_Feng-2) It is used as a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") between people of different linguistic backgrounds.[[473]](#cite_note-langlaw-496) In the [autonomous regions of China](/wiki/Autonomous_regions_of_China "Autonomous regions of China"), other languages may also serve as a lingua franca, such as Uyghur in Xinjiang, where governmental services in Uyghur are constitutionally guaranteed.[[474]](#cite_note-497)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in China](/wiki/Religion_in_China "Religion in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Distribution_of_religions_in_China.png/500px-Distribution_of_religions_in_China.png)](/wiki/File:Distribution_of_religions_in_China.png)

Geographic distribution of religions in China:  
[[475]](#cite_note-map1-498)[[476]](#cite_note-map2-499)[[477]](#cite_note-map3-500)[[478]](#cite_note-map4-501)  
■ [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion") (including [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), and groups of [Chinese Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism"))  
■ [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") *tout court*  
■ [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_China "Islam in China")  
■ [Ethnic minorities' indigenous religions](/wiki/Religion_in_China#Ethnic_minorities'_indigenous_religions "Religion in China")  
■ [Mongolian folk religion](/wiki/Mongolian_folk_religion "Mongolian folk religion")  
■ [Northeast China folk religion](/wiki/Northeast_China_folk_religion "Northeast China folk religion") influenced by Tungus and [Manchu shamanism](/wiki/Manchu_shamanism "Manchu shamanism"); widespread [Shanrendao](/wiki/Shanrendao "Shanrendao")

[Freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion") is guaranteed by China's constitution, although religious organizations that lack official approval can be subject to state persecution.[[197]](#cite_note-Constitution-216) The government of the country is officially [atheist](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism"), and the Chinese Communist Party requires its members to be atheist.[[479]](#cite_note-502) Religious affairs and issues in the country are overseen by the [National Religious Affairs Administration](/wiki/National_Religious_Affairs_Administration "National Religious Affairs Administration"), under the CCP's [United Front Work Department](/wiki/United_Front_Work_Department "United Front Work Department").[[480]](#cite_note-503)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Wudang_Mountain_%2854131067531%29.jpg/250px-Wudang_Mountain_%2854131067531%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wudang_Mountain_(54131067531).jpg)

[Taoist](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") temple on top of [Wudang Mountains](/wiki/Wudang_Mountains "Wudang Mountains") in [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei"), China

Over the millennia, the Chinese civilization has been influenced by various religious movements. The "[three doctrines](/wiki/Three_teachings "Three teachings")" of [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") have historically shaped Chinese culture,[[481]](#cite_note-Yao2011-504)[[482]](#cite_note-505) enriching a [theological and spiritual framework](/wiki/Chinese_theology "Chinese theology") of traditional religion which harks back to the early [Shang](/wiki/Shang "Shang") and [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty"). [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion"), which is framed by the three doctrines and by other traditions,[[483]](#cite_note-506) consists in allegiance to the *[shen](/wiki/Shen_(Chinese_religion) "Shen (Chinese religion)")*, who can be [deities](/wiki/Deities "Deities") of the surrounding nature or [ancestral principles](/wiki/Progenitor "Progenitor") of human groups, concepts of civility, [culture heroes](/wiki/Culture_hero "Culture hero"), many of whom feature in [Chinese mythology](/wiki/Chinese_mythology "Chinese mythology") and history.[[484]](#cite_note-507) Amongst the most popular [cults](/wiki/Cult_(religious_practice) "Cult (religious practice)") of folk religion are those of the [Yellow Emperor](/wiki/Yellow_Emperor "Yellow Emperor"), embodiment of the God of Heaven and one of the two [divine patriarchs](/wiki/Yan_Huang_Zisun "Yan Huang Zisun") of the Chinese people,[[485]](#cite_note-Laliberte2011-508)[[486]](#cite_note-509) of [Mazu](/wiki/Mazu "Mazu") (goddess of the seas),[[485]](#cite_note-Laliberte2011-508) [Guandi](/wiki/Guandi "Guandi") (god of war and business), [Caishen](/wiki/Caishen "Caishen") (god of prosperity and richness), [Pangu](/wiki/Pangu "Pangu") and many others. In the early decades of the 21st century, the Chinese government has been engaged in a rehabilitation of folk cults—formally recognizing them as "folk beliefs" as distinguished from doctrinal religions,[[487]](#cite_note-510) and often reconstructing them into forms of "highly curated" [civil religion](/wiki/Civil_religion "Civil religion")[[488]](#cite_note-511)—as well as in a national and international promotion of Buddhism.[[489]](#cite_note-512) China is home to many of the [world's tallest religious statues](/wiki/List_of_statues_by_height "List of statues by height"), representing either deities of Chinese folk religion or enlightened beings of Buddhism; the tallest of all is the [Spring Temple Buddha](/wiki/Spring_Temple_Buddha "Spring Temple Buddha") in [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E9%81%93%E6%95%99_%E6%8B%9C%E7%AB%A0%E6%98%87%E7%96%8F_01.jpg/250px-%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E9%81%93%E6%95%99_%E6%8B%9C%E7%AB%A0%E6%98%87%E7%96%8F_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E9%81%93%E6%95%99_%E6%8B%9C%E7%AB%A0%E6%98%87%E7%96%8F_01.jpg)

[Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") has served as a state religion several times throughout Chinese history.

Statistics on religious affiliation in China are difficult to gather due to complex and varying definitions of religion and the diffusive nature of Chinese religious traditions. Scholars note that in China there is no clear boundary between the three doctrines and local folk religious practices.[[481]](#cite_note-Yao2011-504) Chinese religions or some of their currents are also definable as [non-theistic](/wiki/Non-theistic "Non-theistic") and [humanistic](/wiki/Humanistic "Humanistic"), since they do not hold that divine creativity is completely transcendent, but that it is inherent in the world and in particular in the human being.[[490]](#cite_note-513) In 2023, according to surveys done by Pew Research, 93% of respondents were formally unaffiliated with any religion. However, in terms of practices, 75% visit family graveyards each year, 47% believe in feng shui, 33% believe in buddha, 26% burn incense to deities each year and 18% believe in taoist deities. These are not exclusive beliefs and often these will overlap as the respondents will have multiple beliefs at the same time. For example, of those 33% who believe in buddha, a significant portion also believe in figures such as Taoist immortals, Jesus Christ, Catholic God and Allah.[[491]](#cite_note-religion2023-514) Chinese folk religion also comprises a variety of [salvationist doctrinal organized movements](/wiki/Chinese_salvationist_religions "Chinese salvationist religions") which emerged since the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty").[[492]](#cite_note-515) There are also ethnic minorities in China who maintain their own [indigenous religions](/wiki/Religion_in_China#Ethnic_minorities'_indigenous_religions "Religion in China"), while major religions characteristic of specific ethnic groups include [Tibetan Buddhism](/wiki/Tibetan_Buddhism "Tibetan Buddhism") among [Tibetans](/wiki/Tibetans "Tibetans"), [Mongols](/wiki/Mongols_in_China "Mongols in China") and [Yugurs](/wiki/Yugurs "Yugurs"),[[493]](#cite_note-516) and Islam among the [Hui](/wiki/Hui "Hui"), [Uyghur](/wiki/Uyghur_people "Uyghur people"), [Kazakh](/wiki/Kazakhs_in_China "Kazakhs in China"),[[494]](#cite_note-:2-517) and [Kyrgyz](/wiki/Kyrgyz_in_China "Kyrgyz in China") peoples, and other ethnicities in the northern and northwestern regions of the country.

### Education

Main articles: [Education in China](/wiki/Education_in_China "Education in China") and [Higher education in China](/wiki/Higher_education_in_China "Higher education in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/13_Peking_University.jpg/250px-13_Peking_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:13_Peking_University.jpg)

Beijing's [Peking University](/wiki/Peking_University "Peking University"), one of the [top-ranked universities in China](/wiki/Rankings_of_universities_in_China "Rankings of universities in China")[[495]](#cite_note-518)[[496]](#cite_note-519)

Compulsory education in China comprises [primary](/wiki/Primary_school "Primary school") and [junior secondary school](/wiki/Middle_school "Middle school"), which together last for nine years from the age of 6 and 15.[[497]](#cite_note-520) The [Gaokao](/wiki/Gaokao "Gaokao"), China's national university entrance exam, is a prerequisite for entrance into most higher education institutions. Vocational education is available to students at the secondary and [tertiary](/wiki/Tertiary_education "Tertiary education") level.[[498]](#cite_note-Ministry_of_Edu_China-2022-521) More than 10 million Chinese students graduated from vocational colleges every year.[[499]](#cite_note-522) In 2024, about 92.0 percent of students continued their education at a three-year senior secondary school, while 60.8 percent of secondary school graduates were enrolled in higher education.[[500]](#cite_note-auto-523)

China has the largest education system in the world,[[501]](#cite_note-UNICEF-2021-524) with about 287 million students and 18.85 million full-time teachers in over 470,300 schools in 2024.[[500]](#cite_note-auto-523) Annual education investment went from less than US$50 billion in 2003 to more than US$817 billion in 2020.[[502]](#cite_note-525)[[503]](#cite_note-526) However, there remains an inequality in education spending. In 2010, the annual education expenditure per secondary school student in Beijing totalled ¥20,023, while in [Guizhou](/wiki/Guizhou "Guizhou"), one of the [poorest provinces](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_provincial-level_divisions_by_GDP_per_capita "List of Chinese provincial-level divisions by GDP per capita"), it only totalled ¥3,204.[[504]](#cite_note-527)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] China's literacy rate has grown dramatically, from only 20% in 1949 and 65.5% in 1979,[[505]](#cite_note-528) to 97% of the population over age 15 in 2020.[[506]](#cite_note-529)

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), China has over 3,167 universities, with over 47.6 million students enrolled in mainland China, giving China the largest higher education system in the world.[[507]](#cite_note-530)[[508]](#cite_note-531) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), China had the world's highest [number of top universities](/wiki/Rankings_of_universities_in_China "Rankings of universities in China").[[509]](#cite_note-532)[[510]](#cite_note-533)[[511]](#cite_note-:03-534) As of 2025, there were 2 Chinese universities in the global top 20, 5 in the top 50, and 19 in the top 200, behind only the United States and the United Kingdom in terms of the overall representation in the *Aggregate Ranking of Top Universities*, a composite ranking system combining three of the world's most influential university rankings ([ARWU](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities")+[QS](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")+[THE](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings")).[[512]](#cite_note-535) China had five universities listed among the world's top 50, placing it third after the United States and the United Kingdom based on aggregate performance from four widely observed university rankings (THE+ARWU+QS+[US News](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report_Best_Global_Universities_Ranking "U.S. News & World Report Best Global Universities Ranking")).[[513]](#cite_note-536) China is home to two of the highest-ranking universities ([Tsinghua University](/wiki/Tsinghua_University "Tsinghua University") and [Peking University](/wiki/Peking_University "Peking University")) in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and [emerging economies](/wiki/Emerging_market "Emerging market"), according to the [Times Higher Education World University Rankings](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings")[[514]](#cite_note-537) and the [Academic Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities").[[515]](#cite_note-538) These universities are members of the [C9 League](/wiki/C9_League "C9 League"), an alliance of elite [Chinese universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_China "List of universities in China") offering comprehensive and leading education.[[516]](#cite_note-539)

### Health

Main article: [Health in China](/wiki/Health_in_China "Health in China")

See also: [Medicine in China](/wiki/Medicine_in_China "Medicine in China") and [Pharmaceutical industry in China](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_industry_in_China "Pharmaceutical industry in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/China%2C_Trends_in_the_Human_Development_Index_1970-2010.png/330px-China%2C_Trends_in_the_Human_Development_Index_1970-2010.png)](/wiki/File:China,_Trends_in_the_Human_Development_Index_1970-2010.png)

Chart showing the rise of China's [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") from 1970 to 2010

The [National Health Commission](/wiki/National_Health_Commission "National Health Commission"), together with its counterparts in the local commissions, oversees the health needs of the population.[[517]](#cite_note-540) An emphasis on public health and preventive medicine has characterized Chinese health policy since the early 1950s. The Communist Party started the [Patriotic Health Campaign](/wiki/Patriotic_Health_Campaign "Patriotic Health Campaign"), which was aimed at improving sanitation and hygiene, as well as treating and preventing several diseases. Diseases such as [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera"), [typhoid](/wiki/Typhoid "Typhoid") and [scarlet fever](/wiki/Scarlet_fever "Scarlet fever"), which were previously rife in China, were nearly eradicated by the campaign.[[518]](#cite_note-541)

After [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") began instituting economic reforms in 1978, the health of the Chinese public improved rapidly because of better nutrition, although many of the free public health services provided in the countryside disappeared. [Healthcare in China](/wiki/Healthcare_in_China "Healthcare in China") became mostly privatized, and experienced a significant rise in quality. In 2009, the government began a three-year large-scale healthcare provision initiative worth US$124 billion.[[519]](#cite_note-542) By 2011, the campaign resulted in 95% of China's population having basic health insurance coverage.[[520]](#cite_note-543) By 2022, China had established itself as a key producer and exporter of [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Medication "Medication"), producing around 40 percent of [active pharmaceutical ingredients](/wiki/Active_ingredient "Active ingredient") in 2017.[[521]](#cite_note-544)

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), the life expectancy at birth exceeds 79 years.[[522]](#cite_note-545) As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit), the [infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") rate is 5 per thousand.[[523]](#cite_note-546) Both have improved significantly since the 1950s.[[v]](#cite_note-549) Rates of [stunting](/wiki/Stunted_growth "Stunted growth"), a condition caused by [malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition"), have declined from 33.1% in 1990 to 4.5% in 2024.[[526]](#cite_note-550)[[527]](#cite_note-551) Despite significant improvements in health and the construction of advanced medical facilities, China has several emerging public health problems, such as respiratory illnesses caused by [widespread air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution_in_China "Air pollution in China"),[[528]](#cite_note-FT-china-pollution-552) [hundreds of millions of cigarette smokers](/wiki/Smoking_in_China "Smoking in China"),[[529]](#cite_note-553) and an increase in [obesity](/wiki/Obesity "Obesity") among urban youths.[[530]](#cite_note-Ref_abcy-554)[[531]](#cite_note-Ref_abcz-555) [Chinese mental health](/wiki/Mental_health_in_China "Mental health in China") services are inadequate.[[532]](#cite_note-556) China's large population and densely populated cities have led to serious disease outbreaks, such as [SARS](/wiki/Severe_acute_respiratory_syndrome "Severe acute respiratory syndrome") in 2003, although this has since been largely contained.[[533]](#cite_note-Ref_abcda-557) The [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") was first identified in [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan "Wuhan") in December 2019;[[534]](#cite_note-auto12-558)[[535]](#cite_note-559) pandemic led the government to enforce [strict public health measures](/wiki/Zero-COVID "Zero-COVID") intended to completely eradicate the virus, a goal that was abandoned in December 2022 following [protests](/wiki/2022_COVID-19_protests_in_China "2022 COVID-19 protests in China").[[536]](#cite_note-560)[[537]](#cite_note-561)

## Culture and society

Main articles: [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture") and [Culture of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Culture_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Culture of the People's Republic of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/%E7%98%A6%E8%A5%BF%E6%B9%96%E5%B0%8F%E9%87%91%E5%B1%B12017.jpg/250px-%E7%98%A6%E8%A5%BF%E6%B9%96%E5%B0%8F%E9%87%91%E5%B1%B12017.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E7%98%A6%E8%A5%BF%E6%B9%96%E5%B0%8F%E9%87%91%E5%B1%B12017.jpg)

A [moon gate](/wiki/Moon_gate "Moon gate") in a Chinese garden

Since [ancient times](/wiki/Ancient_China "Ancient China"), Chinese culture has been heavily influenced by Confucianism. Chinese culture, in turn, has heavily influenced [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").[[538]](#cite_note-562) For much of the country's dynastic era, opportunities for social advancement could be provided by high performance in the prestigious [imperial examinations](/wiki/Imperial_examination "Imperial examination"), which have their origins in the Han dynasty.[[539]](#cite_note-563) The [literary emphasis](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature") of the exams affected the general perception of cultural refinement in China, such as the belief that [calligraphy](/wiki/Chinese_calligraphy "Chinese calligraphy"), [poetry](/wiki/Classical_Chinese_poetry "Classical Chinese poetry") and [painting](/wiki/Chinese_painting "Chinese painting") were higher forms of art than dancing or drama. Chinese culture has long emphasized a sense of deep history and a largely inward-looking national perspective.[[540]](#cite_note-ChinaFuture-564) Examinations and a [culture of merit](/wiki/Meritocracy "Meritocracy") remain greatly valued in China today.[[541]](#cite_note-565)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Fenghuang_old_town.JPG/250px-Fenghuang_old_town.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fenghuang_old_town.JPG)

[Fenghuang County](/wiki/Fenghuang_County "Fenghuang County"), an ancient town that harbors many architectural remains of Ming and Qing styles[[542]](#cite_note-566)

Today, the Chinese government has accepted numerous elements of traditional Chinese culture as being integral to Chinese society. With the rise of [Chinese nationalism](/wiki/Chinese_nationalism "Chinese nationalism") and the end of the Cultural Revolution, various forms of traditional Chinese art, literature, music, film, fashion and architecture have seen a vigorous revival,[[543]](#cite_note-Ref_abcdef-567)[[544]](#cite_note-Ref_abcdeg-568) and folk and variety art in particular have sparked interest nationally and even worldwide.[[545]](#cite_note-Ref_abcdeh-569)

### Architecture

Main articles: [Chinese architecture](/wiki/Chinese_architecture "Chinese architecture") and [List of World Heritage Sites in China](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_China "List of World Heritage Sites in China")

[Chinese architecture](/wiki/Chinese_architecture "Chinese architecture") has developed over millennia in China and has remained a vestigial source of perennial influence on the development of East Asian architecture,[[546]](#cite_note-570)[[547]](#cite_note-571)[[548]](#cite_note-572) including in [Japan](/wiki/Japanese_architecture "Japanese architecture"), [Korea](/wiki/Korean_architecture "Korean architecture"), and [Mongolia](/wiki/Architecture_of_Mongolia "Architecture of Mongolia").[[549]](#cite_note-Cartwright-2023-573) and minor influences on the architecture of Southeast and South Asia including the countries of [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), Thailand, Laos, [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnamese_architecture "Vietnamese architecture") and the Philippines.[[550]](#cite_note-574)[[551]](#cite_note-575)

Chinese architecture is characterized by bilateral symmetry, use of enclosed open spaces, [feng shui](/wiki/Feng_shui "Feng shui") (e.g. directional [hierarchies](/wiki/Hierarchy "Hierarchy")),[[552]](#cite_note-576) a horizontal emphasis, and an allusion to various cosmological, [mythological](/wiki/Myth "Myth") or in general symbolic elements. Chinese architecture traditionally classifies structures according to type, ranging from *[pagodas](/wiki/Pagodas "Pagodas")* to [palaces](/wiki/Chinese_palace "Chinese palace").[[553]](#cite_note-Itō-2017-577)[[549]](#cite_note-Cartwright-2023-573)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Tiananmen_Square_%2854137047250%29.jpg/250px-Tiananmen_Square_%2854137047250%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tiananmen_Square_(54137047250).jpg)

[Tiananmen Square](/wiki/Tiananmen_Square "Tiananmen Square"), a [city square](/wiki/Town_square "Town square") in the [city center](/wiki/City_center "City center") of [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"). [Tiananmen](/wiki/Tiananmen "Tiananmen") is the [entrance gate](/wiki/Gatehouse "Gatehouse") of the [Forbidden City](/wiki/Forbidden_City "Forbidden City").

Chinese architecture varies widely based on status or affiliation, such as whether the structures were constructed for emperors, commoners, or for religious purposes. Other variations in Chinese architecture are shown in vernacular styles associated with different [geographic](/wiki/Geography "Geography") regions and different ethnic heritages, such as the [stilt houses](/wiki/Stilt_house "Stilt house") in the south, the [Yaodong buildings](/wiki/Yaodong "Yaodong") in the northwest, the [yurt buildings](/wiki/Yurt "Yurt") of nomadic people, and the [Siheyuan buildings](/wiki/Siheyuan "Siheyuan") in the north.[[554]](#cite_note-578)

### Literature

Main article: [Chinese literature](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Pekin_przedstawienie_tradycjnego_teatru_chinskiego_7.JPG/250px-Pekin_przedstawienie_tradycjnego_teatru_chinskiego_7.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pekin_przedstawienie_tradycjnego_teatru_chinskiego_7.JPG)

The stories in *[Journey to the West](/wiki/Journey_to_the_West "Journey to the West")* are common themes in [Peking opera](/wiki/Peking_opera "Peking opera").

Chinese literature has its roots in the Zhou dynasty's literary tradition.[[555]](#cite_note-579) The [classical texts of China](/wiki/Chinese_classics "Chinese classics") encompass a wide range of thoughts and subjects, such as the [calendar](/wiki/Chinese_calendar "Chinese calendar"), [military](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_military_texts "List of Chinese military texts"), [astrology](/wiki/Chinese_astrology "Chinese astrology"), [herbology](/wiki/Chinese_herbology "Chinese herbology"), and [geography](/wiki/Chinese_geography "Chinese geography"), as well as many others.[[556]](#cite_note-580) Among the most significant early works are the *[I Ching](/wiki/I_Ching "I Ching")* and the *[Shujing](/wiki/Classic_of_History "Classic of History")*, which are part of the [Four Books and Five Classics](/wiki/Four_Books_and_Five_Classics "Four Books and Five Classics"). These texts were the cornerstone of the Confucian curriculum sponsored by the state throughout the dynastic periods. Inherited from the *[Classic of Poetry](/wiki/Classic_of_Poetry "Classic of Poetry")*, [classical Chinese poetry](/wiki/Classical_Chinese_poetry "Classical Chinese poetry") developed to its [floruit](/wiki/Floruit "Floruit") during the Tang dynasty. [Li Bai](/wiki/Li_Bai "Li Bai") and [Du Fu](/wiki/Du_Fu "Du Fu") opened the forking ways for the poetic circles through romanticism and realism respectively. [Chinese historiography](/wiki/Chinese_historiography "Chinese historiography") began with the *[Shiji](/wiki/Shiji "Shiji")*, the overall scope of the historiographical tradition in China is termed the [Twenty-Four Histories](/wiki/Twenty-Four_Histories "Twenty-Four Histories"), which set a vast stage for Chinese fictions along with [Chinese mythology](/wiki/Chinese_mythology "Chinese mythology") and [folklore](/wiki/Chinese_folklore "Chinese folklore").[[557]](#cite_note-581) Pushed by a burgeoning citizen class in the Ming dynasty, Chinese classical fiction rose to a boom of the historical, town and [gods and demons fictions](/wiki/Gods_and_demons_fiction "Gods and demons fiction") as represented by the [Four Great Classical Novels](/wiki/Four_Great_Classical_Novels "Four Great Classical Novels") which include *[Water Margin](/wiki/Water_Margin "Water Margin")*, *[Romance of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Romance_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Romance of the Three Kingdoms")*, *[Journey to the West](/wiki/Journey_to_the_West "Journey to the West")* and *[Dream of the Red Chamber](/wiki/Dream_of_the_Red_Chamber "Dream of the Red Chamber")*.[[558]](#cite_note-582) Along with the [wuxia](/wiki/Wuxia "Wuxia") fictions of [Jin Yong](/wiki/Jin_Yong "Jin Yong") and [Liang Yusheng](/wiki/Liang_Yusheng "Liang Yusheng"),[[559]](#cite_note-583) it remains an enduring source of popular culture in the [Chinese sphere of influence](/wiki/Sinosphere "Sinosphere").[[560]](#cite_note-584)

In the wake of the [New Culture Movement](/wiki/New_Culture_Movement "New Culture Movement") after the end of the Qing dynasty, Chinese literature embarked on a new era with [written vernacular Chinese](/wiki/Written_vernacular_Chinese "Written vernacular Chinese") for ordinary citizens. [Hu Shih](/wiki/Hu_Shih "Hu Shih") and [Lu Xun](/wiki/Lu_Xun "Lu Xun") were pioneers in modern literature.[[561]](#cite_note-585) Various literary genres, such as [misty poetry](/wiki/Misty_poetry "Misty poetry"), [scar literature](/wiki/Scar_literature "Scar literature"), [young adult fiction](/wiki/Young_adult_fiction "Young adult fiction") and the [xungen literature](/wiki/Xungen_movement "Xungen movement"), which is influenced by [magic realism](/wiki/Magic_realism "Magic realism"),[[562]](#cite_note-586) emerged following the Cultural Revolution. [Mo Yan](/wiki/Mo_Yan "Mo Yan"), a xungen literature author, was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2012.[[563]](#cite_note-587)

### Music

Main articles: [Music of China](/wiki/Music_of_China "Music of China"), [C-pop](/wiki/C-pop "C-pop"), and [Chinese opera](/wiki/Chinese_opera "Chinese opera")

Chinese music covers a highly diverse range of music from traditional music to modern music. Chinese music dates back before the pre-imperial times. [Traditional Chinese musical instruments](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_musical_instruments "Traditional Chinese musical instruments") were traditionally grouped into eight categories known as *bayin* (八音). Traditional Chinese opera is a form of musical theatre in China originating thousands of years and has regional style forms such as Beijing and [Cantonese opera](/wiki/Cantonese_opera "Cantonese opera").[[564]](#cite_note-588) Chinese pop (C-Pop) includes [mandopop](/wiki/Mandopop "Mandopop") and [cantopop](/wiki/Cantopop "Cantopop"). [Chinese hip hop](/wiki/Chinese_hip_hop "Chinese hip hop") and [Hong Kong hip hop](/wiki/Hong_Kong_hip_hop "Hong Kong hip hop") have become popular.[[565]](#cite_note-589)

### Fashion

Main articles: [Chinese clothing](/wiki/Chinese_clothing "Chinese clothing") and [Hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/%E6%9B%B2%E9%98%9C%E5%B8%AB%E7%AF%84%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E3%81%AE%E6%B4%99%E6%B3%97%E9%83%A8%E3%81%AE%E5%86%99%E7%9C%9F.jpg/250px-%E6%9B%B2%E9%98%9C%E5%B8%AB%E7%AF%84%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E3%81%AE%E6%B4%99%E6%B3%97%E9%83%A8%E3%81%AE%E5%86%99%E7%9C%9F.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%9B%B2%E9%98%9C%E5%B8%AB%E7%AF%84%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E3%81%AE%E6%B4%99%E6%B3%97%E9%83%A8%E3%81%AE%E5%86%99%E7%9C%9F.jpg)

Young women wearing [Hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu") at the [Qufu Normal University](/wiki/Qufu_Normal_University "Qufu Normal University"), [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong")

[Hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu") is the historical clothing of the Han people in China. The [qipao](/wiki/Cheongsam "Cheongsam") or cheongsam is a popular Chinese female dress.[[566]](#cite_note-590) The [hanfu movement](/wiki/Hanfu_movement "Hanfu movement") has been popular in contemporary times and seeks to revitalize Hanfu clothing.[[567]](#cite_note-591) [China Fashion Week](/wiki/China_Fashion_Week "China Fashion Week") is the country's only national-level fashion festival.[[568]](#cite_note-592)

### Media

Main articles: [Mass media in China](/wiki/Mass_media_in_China "Mass media in China") and [Cinema of China](/wiki/Cinema_of_China "Cinema of China")

The mass media of China primarily consists of [television](/wiki/Television_in_China "Television in China"), [newspapers](/wiki/Newspapers_of_China "Newspapers of China"), [radio](/wiki/Radio_in_China "Radio in China"), and [magazines](/wiki/List_of_magazines_in_China "List of magazines in China"). [State media](/wiki/State_media "State media") outlets operate under the control of the CCP. The largest media organizations are the *[People's Daily](/wiki/People%27s_Daily "People's Daily")*, [Xinhua News Agency](/wiki/Xinhua_News_Agency "Xinhua News Agency"), and the [China Media Group](/wiki/China_Media_Group "China Media Group") consisting of [China Central Television](/wiki/China_Central_Television "China Central Television"), [China Global Television Network](/wiki/China_Global_Television_Network "China Global Television Network"), [China National Radio](/wiki/China_National_Radio "China National Radio") and [China Radio International](/wiki/China_Radio_International "China Radio International").[[569]](#cite_note-593) Access to foreign media remains heavily restricted.[[570]](#cite_note-594)

Cinema was first introduced to China in 1896 and the first Chinese film, *[Dingjun Mountain](/wiki/Dingjun_Mountain_(film) "Dingjun Mountain (film)"),* was released in 1905.[[571]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia-595) China has had the largest number of movie screens in the world since 2016;[[572]](#cite_note-hr-596) China became the largest cinema market in 2020,[[573]](#cite_note-597)[[574]](#cite_note-598) and domestic movies dominate the market.[[575]](#cite_note-599) The top three [highest-grossing films in China](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films_in_China "List of highest-grossing films in China") as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit) were *[Ne Zha 2](/wiki/Ne_Zha_2 "Ne Zha 2")* (2025), *[The Battle at Lake Changjin](/wiki/The_Battle_at_Lake_Changjin "The Battle at Lake Changjin")* (2021), and *[Wolf Warrior 2](/wiki/Wolf_Warrior_2 "Wolf Warrior 2")* (2017).[[576]](#cite_note-Alltimedomestic-600) In 2025, the video game market of China was the world's [largest by revenue](/wiki/List_of_video_games_markets_by_country "List of video games markets by country").[[577]](#cite_note-601)

China has the most comprehensive and sophisticated Internet censorship regime in the world called the [Great Firewall](/wiki/Great_Firewall "Great Firewall"), with numerous websites being blocked.[[578]](#cite_note-602) The Great Firewall has allowed China to develop its own major internet services and companies, such as [Tencent](/wiki/Tencent "Tencent"), [Alibaba](/wiki/Alibaba_Group "Alibaba Group"), [Baidu](/wiki/Baidu "Baidu"), [Douyin](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok"), [Xiaohongshu](/wiki/Xiaohongshu "Xiaohongshu"), [Bilibili](/wiki/Bilibili "Bilibili") and [Weibo](/wiki/Weibo "Weibo").[[579]](#cite_note-:122-603): 8 The [Cyberspace Administration of China](/wiki/Cyberspace_Administration_of_China "Cyberspace Administration of China") acts as the national internet regulator and censor.[[580]](#cite_note-:42-604) China requires a [real-name system](/wiki/Internet_real-name_system_in_China "Internet real-name system in China") for Internet services and online platforms.[[581]](#cite_note-:3-605)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Chinese cuisine](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Cuisines_of_China.png/250px-Cuisines_of_China.png)](/wiki/File:Cuisines_of_China.png)

Map showing major regional cuisines of China

Chinese cuisine is highly diverse, drawing on several millennia of culinary history and geographical variety, in which the most influential are known as the "Eight Major Cuisines", including [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan_cuisine "Sichuan cuisine"), [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese_cuisine "Cantonese cuisine"), [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu_cuisine "Jiangsu cuisine"), [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong_cuisine "Shandong cuisine"), [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian_cuisine "Fujian cuisine"), [Hunan](/wiki/Hunan_cuisine "Hunan cuisine"), [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui_cuisine "Anhui cuisine"), and [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang_cuisine "Zhejiang cuisine") cuisines.[[582]](#cite_note-606) Chinese cuisine is known for its breadth of [cooking methods](/wiki/Chinese_cooking_techniques "Chinese cooking techniques") and ingredients.[[583]](#cite_note-607) China's staple food is rice in the northeast and south, and wheat-based breads and noodles in the north. Bean products such as [tofu](/wiki/Tofu "Tofu") and [soy milk](/wiki/Soy_milk "Soy milk") remain a popular source of protein. Pork is now the most popular meat in China, accounting for about 60% of the country's total meat consumption.[[584]](#cite_note-608) Pork-free [Chinese Islamic cuisine](/wiki/Chinese_Islamic_cuisine "Chinese Islamic cuisine") is served at halal-certified restaurants in various regions[[585]](#cite_note-609) while vegetarian [Buddhist cuisine](/wiki/Buddhist_cuisine "Buddhist cuisine") is commonly found at restaurants near shrines and temples.[[586]](#cite_note-610)[[587]](#cite_note-611)

### Sports

Main articles: [Sport in China](/wiki/Sport_in_China "Sport in China"), [China at the Olympics](/wiki/China_at_the_Olympics "China at the Olympics"), and [China at the Paralympics](/wiki/China_at_the_Paralympics "China at the Paralympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/FloorGoban.JPG/250px-FloorGoban.JPG)](/wiki/File:FloorGoban.JPG)

[Go](/wiki/Go_(game) "Go (game)") is an abstract strategy board game for two players, in which the aim is to surround more territory than the opponent, and which was invented in China more than 2,500 years ago.

China has one of the [oldest sporting cultures](/wiki/Sport_in_China "Sport in China"). There is evidence that [archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery") (*shèjiàn*) was practiced during the [Western Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Western_Zhou_dynasty "Western Zhou dynasty"). Swordplay (*jiànshù*) and *[cuju](/wiki/Cuju "Cuju")*, a sport loosely related to [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football")[[588]](#cite_note-612) date back to China's early dynasties as well.[[589]](#cite_note-613)

[Physical fitness](/wiki/Physical_fitness "Physical fitness") is widely emphasized in Chinese culture, with morning exercises such as *[qigong](/wiki/Qigong "Qigong")* and [tai chi](/wiki/Tai_chi "Tai chi") widely practiced,[[590]](#cite_note-614) and commercial [gyms](/wiki/Gym "Gym") and private fitness clubs are gaining popularity.[[591]](#cite_note-615) Basketball is the most popular spectator sport in China.[[592]](#cite_note-616) The [Chinese Basketball Association](/wiki/Chinese_Basketball_Association "Chinese Basketball Association") and the American [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") also have a huge national following amongst the Chinese populace, with native-born and NBA-bound Chinese players and well-known national household names such as [Yao Ming](/wiki/Yao_Ming "Yao Ming") and [Yi Jianlian](/wiki/Yi_Jianlian "Yi Jianlian") being held in high esteem.[[593]](#cite_note-Beech2003-617) China's professional football league, known as [Chinese Super League](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League"), is the largest football market in East Asia.[[594]](#cite_note-618) Other popular sports include [martial arts](/wiki/Chinese_martial_arts "Chinese martial arts"), [table tennis](/wiki/Table_tennis "Table tennis"), [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)") and [snooker](/wiki/Snooker "Snooker"). China is home to a huge number of [cyclists](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling"), with an estimated 470 million bicycles as of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=China&action=edit).[[595]](#cite_note-470MBikes-619) China has the world's largest [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") market.[[596]](#cite_note-620) Many more [traditional sports](/wiki/Traditional_games_of_China "Traditional games of China"), such as [dragon boat](/wiki/Dragon_boat "Dragon boat") racing, [Mongolian-style wrestling](/wiki/Mongolian_wrestling "Mongolian wrestling")[[597]](#cite_note-621)[[598]](#cite_note-622) and [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") are also popular.

China has [participated in the Olympic Games](/wiki/China_at_the_Olympics "China at the Olympics") since 1932, although it has only participated as the PRC [since 1952](/wiki/China_at_the_1952_Summer_Olympics "China at the 1952 Summer Olympics"). China hosted the [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics") in Beijing, where its athletes received 48 gold medals – the [highest number of any participating nation that year](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics_medal_table "2008 Summer Olympics medal table").[[599]](#cite_note-Ref_abcdeo-623) China also won the most medals at the [2012 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Paralympics "2012 Summer Paralympics"), with 231 overall, including 95 gold.[[600]](#cite_note-624)[[601]](#cite_note-625) In 2011, Shenzhen hosted the [2011 Summer Universiade](/wiki/2011_Summer_Universiade "2011 Summer Universiade"). China hosted the [2013 East Asian Games](/wiki/2013_East_Asian_Games "2013 East Asian Games") in Tianjin and the [2014 Summer Youth Olympics](/wiki/2014_Summer_Youth_Olympics "2014 Summer Youth Olympics") in Nanjing, the first country to host both regular and Youth Olympics. Beijing and its nearby city [Zhangjiakou](/wiki/Zhangjiakou "Zhangjiakou") collaboratively hosted the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2022_Winter_Olympics "2022 Winter Olympics"), making Beijing the first dual Olympic city by holding both the Summer Olympics and the Winter Olympics.[[602]](#cite_note-626) China hosted the [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games") in [1990](/wiki/1990_Asian_Games "1990 Asian Games") (Beijing), [2010](/wiki/2010_Asian_Games "2010 Asian Games") (Guangzhou), and [2023](/wiki/2022_Asian_Games "2022 Asian Games") (Hangzhou).[[603]](#cite_note-627)

8. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** [Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 中国; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Zhōngguó*
9. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** [Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 中华人民共和国; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Zhōnghuá Rénmín Gònghéguó*
10. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** China's border with Pakistan is disputed by India, which claims the entire [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") region as its territory. China is tied with Russia as having the [most land borders of any country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_by_number_of_land_borders "List of countries and territories by number of land borders").
11. **[^](#cite_ref-24)** The total area ranking relative to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") depends on the measurement of total areas. See [list of countries and dependencies by area](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area") for information. The following two primary sources represent the range of estimates of China's and the United States' total areas.
    1. The *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")* lists China as the third-largest country (after Russia and Canada) with a total area of 9,572,900 km2,[[6]](#cite_note-britannica-9) and the US as fourth-largest at 9,525,067 km2.[[12]](#cite_note-United_States-22)
    2. The [CIA *World Factbook*](/wiki/The_World_Factbook "The World Factbook") lists China as the fourth-largest country (after Russia, Canada and the United States) with a total area of 9,596,960 km2,[[4]](#cite_note-CIA-7) and the United States as the third-largest at 9,833,517 km2.[[13]](#cite_note-23)Both sources exclude both Taiwan and coastal and territorial waters from the area of China. However, the CIA *World Factbook* includes the United States coastal and territorial waters, while Encyclopædia Britannica excludes them. Britannica specifies the United States' area (excluding coastal and territorial waters) as 9,525,067 km2, which is less than either source's figure given for China's area.[[12]](#cite_note-United_States-22) The [United Nations Statistics Division](/wiki/United_Nations_Statistics_Division "United Nations Statistics Division")'s figure for the US is 9,833,517 km2 (3,796,742 sq mi) and China is 9,596,961 km2 (3,705,407 sq mi). These closely match the CIA *World Factbook* figures and similarly *include* coastal and territorial waters for the United States, but *exclude* coastal and territorial waters for China.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-25)** Excluding the disputed [Taiwan Province](/wiki/Taiwan_Province,_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Taiwan Province, People's Republic of China"). See [§ Administrative divisions](#Administrative_divisions).
13. **[^](#cite_ref-29)** "... Next into this, is found the great China, whose king is thought to be the greatest prince in the world, and is named Santoa Raia".[[15]](#cite_note-27)[[16]](#cite_note-28)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-32)** "... The Very Great Kingdom of China".[[17]](#cite_note-30) ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *... O Grande Reino da China ...*).[[18]](#cite_note-31)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-39)** Its earliest extant use is on the [ritual bronze](/wiki/Ritual_bronze "Ritual bronze") vessel [He zun](/wiki/He_zun "He zun"), where it apparently refers to only the [Shang](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty")'s immediate demesne conquered by the [Zhou](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty").[[24]](#cite_note-38)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-41)** Its meaning "Zhou's royal demesne" is attested from the 6th-century BC [Classic of History](/wiki/Classic_of_History "Classic of History"), which states "[Huangtian](/wiki/Tian_(god) "Tian (god)") bestowed the lands and the peoples of the central state to the ancestors" (皇天既付中國民越厥疆土于先王).[[25]](#cite_note-40)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-67)** Owing to Qin Shi Huang's earlier policy involving the "[burning of books and burying of scholars](/wiki/Burning_of_books_and_burying_of_scholars "Burning of books and burying of scholars")", the destruction of the confiscated copies at [Xianyang](/wiki/Xianyang "Xianyang") was an event similar to the [destructions](/wiki/Destruction_of_the_Library_of_Alexandria "Destruction of the Library of Alexandria") of the [Library of Alexandria](/wiki/Library_of_Alexandria "Library of Alexandria") in the west. Even those texts that did survive had to be painstakingly reconstructed from memory, luck, or forgery.[[50]](#cite_note-66) The [Old Texts](/wiki/Old_Texts "Old Texts") of the [Five Classics](/wiki/Five_Classics "Five Classics") were said to have been found hidden in a wall at the Kong residence in [Qufu](/wiki/Qufu "Qufu"). [Mei Ze](/wiki/Mei_Ze "Mei Ze")'s "rediscovered" edition of the [Book of Documents](/wiki/Book_of_Documents "Book of Documents") was [only shown to be a forgery in the Qing dynasty](/wiki/Yan_Ruoqu "Yan Ruoqu").
18. **[^](#cite_ref-190)** According to the *Encyclopædia Britannica*, the total area of the United States, at 9,522,055 km2 (3,676,486 sq mi), is slightly smaller than that of China. Meanwhile, the *CIA World Factbook* states that China's total area was greater than that of the United States until the coastal waters of the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") was added to the United States' total area in 1996. From 1989 through 1996, the total area of US was listed as 9,372,610 km2 (3,618,780 sq mi) (land area plus inland water only). The listed total area changed to 9,629,091 km2 (3,717,813 sq mi) in 1997 (with the Great Lakes areas and the coastal waters added), to 9,631,418 km2 (3,718,711 sq mi) in 2004, to 9,631,420 km2 (3,718,710 sq mi) in 2006, and to 9,826,630 km2 (3,794,080 sq mi) in 2007 (territorial waters added).
19. **[^](#cite_ref-192)** China's border with Pakistan and part of its border with India falls in the disputed region of [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir"). The area under Pakistani administration is claimed by India, while the area under Indian administration is claimed by Pakistan.
20. **[^](#cite_ref-TaiwanClaim_224-0)** The People's Republic of China claims the islands of [Taiwan](/wiki/Geography_of_Taiwan "Geography of Taiwan") and [Penghu](/wiki/Penghu "Penghu"), which it does not control, as its disputed 23rd province, i.e. [Taiwan Province](/wiki/Taiwan_Province,_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Taiwan Province, People's Republic of China"); along with [Kinmen](/wiki/Kinmen "Kinmen") and [Matsu Islands](/wiki/Matsu_Islands "Matsu Islands") as part of [Fujian Province](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"). These are controlled by the Taipei-based [Republic of China](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") (ROC). See [§ Administrative divisions](#Administrative_divisions) for more details.
21. **[^](#cite_ref-385)** Some of the chips used were not domestically developed until [Sunway TaihuLight](/wiki/Sunway_TaihuLight "Sunway TaihuLight") in 2016. China [has not submitted](/wiki/TOP500#Large_machines_not_on_the_list "TOP500") newer entries to TOP500 amid tensions with the United States.
22. **[^](#cite_ref-549)** The national life expectancy at birth rose from about 31 years in 1949 to 75 years in 2008,[[524]](#cite_note-547) and infant mortality decreased from 300 per thousand in the 1950s to around 33 per thousand in 2001.[[525]](#cite_note-Ref_abcu-548)
